Additionally, we are investigating the role of the larynx in another motor neuron disease affecting both children and adults. Lisa project aligns with my ongoing areas of research. My research has particularly focused on the role of the upper airways during these techniques, with the goal of enhancing respiratory care for patients, and I consider myself to be very suitable support for Lisa’s project. I am currently one of the world's leading physiotherapists in the field of chronic respiratory failure, including serving as the physiotherapy representative in a small expert group that organizes the multidisciplinary World Congress on Home Mechanical Ventilation (JIVD Congress), as well as the "Respiratory Failure and Mechanical Ventilation" course under the auspices of the world's largest respiratory organization, the European Respiratory Society (ERS). Through many years of work in an international environment, I have also developed a significant interdisciplinary network across several countries, where we actively collaborate on everything from clinical reasoning to drafting international guidelines. After my PhD defence in 2018, I took on my first roles as a co- supervisor for two PhD students who successfully defended their theses in 2022 and 2024. Currently, I lead my own research group with four active PhD students, for whom I serve as the primary supervisor. This includes three physiotherapists and one medical doctor specializing in pulmonology. Additionally, I have supervised 10 master's students who have successfully completed their degrees and am currently supervising two additional master's students. Further, I have engaged as co-supervisor for Lisa Edel in her project in the planning phase. Full Name Dr Ema Swingwood Organisation UNIVERSITY HOSPITALS BRISTOL AND WESTON NHS FOUNDATION TRUST Email Address Ema.Swingwood@uwe.ac.uk Role Practice Supervisor Description of Support Alongside my clinical consultant role, I am a Clinical Research Fellow at Provided the University of the West of England (UWE) and a visiting lecturer at UWE and University College London for undergraduate and post- graduate Physiotherapy programmes. I have 20 years of clinical experience specifically in respiratory (both adults and paediatrics) and have been extensively involved in cough augmentation and airway clearance using the mechanical insufflation- exsufflation device, so have an excellent understanding of the clinical area that Lisa wishes to explore. I have lectured at International conferences over a number of years around these topics. Having completed my MSc in 2012, I have continued my research with my PhD work, which explored the use of MI-E in the intubated population. I currently hold an NIHR SCPRA award 2025-2027. I am the research lead for Diagnostics and Therapies at UHBW where a key component of my role is embedding research into the clinical setting and developing a research infrastructure for non-medics. My experiences with this will enable me to work effectively with Lisa during and beyond her PhD to develop a clinical academic role. I have supervised 9 MSc students (University College London) to completion, am supervising numerous ICA Internship applications, and am named as a clinical academic supervisor in another doctoral application. I have known Lisa for approximately five years, initially in a clinical capacity and through links within key national respiratory stakeholder groups, and more recently through informal mentoring. Lisa is a national expert in her field of the respiratory management of neuromuscular paediatrics patients. She has sought out opportunities to initially develop her research capability and research network. She is dedicated to optimising the care of her patients and her own development and I have no doubt that she will excel in this fellowship. I am thrilled to be able to support her and look forward to working with Lisa on this fascinating programme. Full Name Professor Giovanni Baranello Organisation University College London Email Address g.baranello@ucl.ac.uk Role Primary Academic Supervisor Description of Support I have had the opportunity to work closely with Lisa over the past 6 Provided years as neuromuscular consultant at the Great Ormond Street Hospital and we have collaborated on several projects, like the correlation between a clinical respiratory score and a more objective respiratory assessment using the optoelectronic plethysmography. Lisa has a strong interest in developing research in the respiratory management of children with neuromuscular diseases and has brilliant ideas on how to demonstrate that the respiratory physiotherapy management of airway clearance can lead to improved respiratory status of children with Spinal Muscular Atrophy (SMA). Lisa has shown herself to be intellectually excellent, hardworking, well- organized, and has a keen and enquiring mind. She has excellent written and oral presentation skills and interacts absolutely well with colleagues. She has authored a few publications, she is actively involved in national and international research networks, like the International SMA Consortium, that include research centres from the UK, Italy and the US, and has presented her research projects at national and international conferences. I am committed to provide Lisa with all the necessary supervision during her PhD project, should she be successful in her application. I have the time and expertise to do so. As clinician academic I regularly provide supervision to postgraduate students. In the last 5 years I have supervised 5 PhD students, 2 as primary, and 2 have achieved completion. The mission of the UCL Great Ormond Street Institute of Child Health where I am based is to improve the health and well-being of children, and the adults they will become, through world-class research, education and public engagement. The UCL GOS ICH, together with its clinical partner Great Ormond Street Hospital for Children, forms the largest concentration of children’s health research outside North America. The Dubowitz Neuromuscular Centre is the largest paediatric neuromuscular centre in the UK, and has a strong research interest in the causes and treatment of muscular dystrophies and other neuromuscular disorders in children. Along with myself, Professor Francesco Muntoni is also an academic member and lead of The Dubowitz Neuromuscular Centre. There are also other research members of the team that can provide support for Lisa’s research project, including 3 research physiotherapists and 3 clinical research fellows. I recommend Lisa Edel unreservedly for this PhD application. She is outstanding in terms of her personal and intellectual attributes. I am sure that she will be an excellent PhD student and will progress to a successful clinical-academic career. Full Name Dr Colin Butler Organisation GREAT ORMOND STREET HOSPITAL FOR CHILDREN NHS FOUNDATION TRUST Email Address colin.butler@gosh.nhs.uk Role Academic Supervisor Description of Support I am delighted to write in support Lisa Edel and her application. The Provided research proposal has the potential to significantly advance the care of children with Spinal Muscular Atrophy (SMA), where the focus of the research will be on understanding upper airway dynamicsechniques can be applied to improve mechanical insufflation and exsufflation in these children to improve and how novel t respiratory health. I am an academic ENT surgeon at Great Ormond Street Hospital (GOSH)and a senior clinical lecturer and PI of the EpiCENTR group at the Institute of Child Health at UCL. I have known Lisa for 2 years, both clinically and from a research perspective. At GOSH, she is a specialist in treating children with SMA, and her academic focus will undoubtedly make her a future leader in her field. The research proposal has the potential for high impact in improving respiratory health, not only in children with SMA but also has application to other pathologies. Lisa is in a unique position at Great Ormond Street to investigate children with this severe airway disease and has an excellent research track record to date. In relation to this proposal, I am pleased to offer assistance in supervising the clinical application of the endoscopy techniques proposed in the work and will support Lisa with the analysis and research methodologies outlined. Lisa will have access to the Ear Nose and Throat (ENT) research group at ICH, where there are weekly research group meetings with airway-based research clinicians.. The Institue of Child Health will provide Lisa appropriate postdoctoral research support, with appropriate training in place. My research group at ICH focuses on paediatric ENT research (clinical and laboratory based), where we have an active group of PhD and MSc students. I currently co-supervise 2 PhD students and 2 MSc students. I have completed supervision on 1 PhD student (completed within 4 years) and 2 MD students where they were awarded their degrees respectively. I also currently co-supervise a postdoctoral NIHR clinical lecturer in paediatric ENT. If awarded, Lisa will have regular supervision and be able gain peer support from the group of doctoral students linked to our group. A benefit of the cross-disciplinary approach will mean she will have support from several allied specialities. As an ENT consultant specialising in airway research, I can offer clinical training to Lisa in the endoscopy techniques required for the proposal and provide supportive analysis of the proposed in application. Our group has experience in both quantitative and As an ENT consultant specialising in airway research, I can provide clinical training to Lisa in the endoscopy techniques required for the proposal and offer supportive analysis of the proposed application. Our group has experience in both quantitative and qualitative research and in developing validated outcomes to assess airway pathologies. In short, I am enthusiastic about supporting Lisa and this project, which I believe has the potential to enhance healthcare, not only in the targeted area but also for children with significant respiratory diseases in general. Full Name Ms Deborah Ridout Organisation UCL Great Ormond Street Institute of Child Health Email Address d.ridout@ucl.ac.uk Role Academic Supervisor Description of Support As Director of the statistical support service at the Institute of Child Provided Health, I have more than 20 years of experience collaborating with colleagues throughout the Institute, UCL and with external academic and industry partners. This has allowed me the opportunity to supervise and oversee many PhD and MSc students. In the last 6 years I have supervised 2 PhD students to completion and have been part of the supervisory panel for 6 others. One of my main areas of research is paediatric neuromuscular disease and I have collaborated extensively with experts in the field and have co-authored over 30 related publications. I have expertise in respiratory outcome measures in the patient population in the project and have supported many colleagues and students in understanding this type of data from a statistical perspective. I have helped design and set up many successful studies and am experienced in supporting and guiding students to develop their research skills. I am a statistics expert and can guide Lisa in her training and support her in applying her newly developed skills to her project. I have known Lisa for more than 6 years and found her to be a dedicated researcher who is keen and ambitious to explore new areas of research. I am committed to providing Lisa with all the necessary supervision during her PhD project, should she be successful in her application and have no doubt she will be an excellent student. Full Name Professor Dr Faith Gibson Organisation GREAT ORMOND STREET HOSPITAL FOR CHILDREN NHS FOUNDATION TRUST Email Address faith.gibson@gosh.nhs.uk Role Academic Supervisor Description of Support Lisa and I have worked together in terms of clinical academic activities Provided across the trust over the last 5 years. I am aware of Lisa’s profile and activities in research. I feel she is ready for a fellowship like this one and she is ready to move along in her chosen career of academic clinical research. She is ready to lead more research and support others in their research development. There is a lot of support for Lisa. She joins a thriving academic community. ORCHID is a research centre; Lisa is part of this centre and therefore benefits through interactions with PhD prepared nurses and Allied Health Professionals: in the clinical academic faculty we currently support 16 DCAF students, 5 PCAFs, 10 interns, and 13 post-docs, 1ACAF. We have a regular programme of activities for students, bringing them together with the Research Faculty: researchers on time- limited studies, that includes social scientists and health service researchers. We receive some support from the GOSH BRC and have an established vibrant internship programme and have huge success with the various NIHR programmes. My role as Director of this Centre is to actively support nurses and allied health professionals on their academic pathway, whoever is funding their fellowship, as well as supporting and mentoring other researchers within the Centre. To date I have been part of16 supervisory teams, I was primary supervisor for 7 of these: all successful and timely completions. I currently have 3 PhD students. I have undertaken 28 PhD vivas. My research expertise is qualitative and uses creative approaches to work with young children. My main field of research is children and young people who have cancer. I am a children’s cancer nurse with over 30 years’ experience in this speciality. My specific role is to support Lisa with her study design and working to develop questions to be used with families in finding out the tolerance, acceptability and comfort when receiving the intervention. Where there are no validated scales available, we have worked with PPI and communities, that includes healthcare professionals to devise suitable questions that help us to understand patient and family experiences. Families with lived experience in particular can help us to find the right questions, the right way to ask and when to ask. We have published some of this work, see for example: Kate Oulton, Naomi Oldrieve, Julie Bayliss, Victoria Jones, Isobel Manning, Lisa Shipway & Faith Gibson (2017): Using participatory and creative research methods to develop and pilot an informative game for preparing children for blood tests, Arts & Health, DOI:10.1080/17533015.2017.1392329

sub_criteria.name: Trajectory Towards Research Leadership
sub_criteria.definition: Demonstrates potential and progression towards becoming a future leader in health or social care research.

signals.signal_text: Statements of career ambitions and long-term goals
Evidence of increasing responsibility in previous roles (e.g., leading projects, mentoring)
Participation in leadership or professional development programs
Recognition by peers or institutions suggesting leadership potential

sub_criteria.pros: Supervisors explicitly state the applicant is ready to lead more research and support others (g.5.a, g.5.b). Recognition by peers (g.5.d) is strong, with descriptions of her as a 'national expert' and 'outstanding'. Participation in leadership programs (g.5.c) is evidenced by the planned Kings Fund leadership course.
sub_criteria.drawbacks: While the potential for leadership is clear, the evidence of *current* leadership in previous roles (g.5.b) is described more as 'contributing' and 'participating' in networks rather than leading large-scale independent projects, though the trajectory is evident.
sub_criteria.rationale: While the potential for leadership is clear, the evidence of *current* leadership in previous roles (g.5.b) is described more as 'contributing' and 'participating' in networks rather than leading large-scale independent projects, though the trajectory is evident.

Her innovative approach to clinical practice and research in this niche area has gained international recognition and she has significantly contributed to improving patient care. Her dedication to translational research is evidenced by the implementation of her respiratory assessment tool in clinical settings worldwide, particularly for children with Spinal Muscular Atrophy. Her academic achievements, such as receiving the Lea Rose Award, further underscore her research capabilities. Additionally, her extensive international collaborations reflect her commitment to advancing standards of care and her ability to engage effectively with a global research community. I will support Lisa with the method of continuous laryngoscopy during the MI-E interventions, how to plan the examinations, perform them, and how to interpret the data, which is a method I developed in 2010. I developed the idea to use laryngoscopy to study the upper airways during dynamic physiotherapy treatment with a cough assist device. My PhD involved assessing the feasibility of the examination and determining normal findings in healthy individuals. Subsequently, I examined all Amyotrophic Lateral Sclerosis patients in our region and identified abnormalities in these complex cases. sub_criteria.name: Commitment to Practitioner-Academic Career (if applicable)
sub_criteria.definition: For applicants including a clinical or practice element, shows a clear commitment to combining research with professional practice.

signals.signal_text: Explicit statement of practitioner-academic career intentions
Involvement in clinical or practice-based research
Evidence of balancing practice responsibilities with research activities
Participation in relevant professional networks or qualifications

sub_criteria.pros: There is an explicit commitment to a practitioner-academic career (g.6.a) with supervisors supporting the development of a clinical-academic role. The applicant is a specialist physiotherapist at GOSH (g.6.b) and is actively involved in clinical research. Participation in professional networks (g.6.d) is extensive, including the International SMA Consortium.
sub_criteria.drawbacks: Evidence of balancing practice responsibilities with research activities (g.6.c) is implied through the clinical role but not explicitly detailed in terms of time management or specific strategies for balancing the two during the PhD.
sub_criteria.rationale: Evidence of balancing practice responsibilities with research activities (g.6.c) is implied through the clinical role but not explicitly detailed in terms of time management or specific strategies for balancing the two during the PhD.

Additionally, I will contribute for Lisa to visit us and learn the method of transnasal fibreoptic laryngoscopy during MI-E manoeuvres from the research group that is currently world leader on this. I will support Lisa with the method of continuous laryngoscopy during the MI-E interventions, how to plan the examinations, perform them, and how to interpret the data, which is a method I developed in 2010. I developed the idea to use laryngoscopy to study the upper airways during dynamic physiotherapy treatment with a cough assist device. My PhD involved assessing the feasibility of the examination and determining normal findings in healthy individuals. Subsequently, I examined all Amyotrophic Lateral Sclerosis patients in our region and identified abnormalities in these complex cases. This was followed up to five years throughout disease progression, describing how these changes evolve over time. This research has significantly improved the clinical treatment of the most vulnerable and challenging patients with chronic respiratory failure. Following my PhD (2018), I continued working as a postdoctoral researcher with a 50% grant in 5 years from Western Norway Health Authorities, focusing on enhancing clinical respiratory support treatment for ALS patients. This multicentre study includes participants from Bergen, Norway, and Porto, Portugal. Additionally, we are investigating the role of the larynx in another motor neuron disease affecting both children and adults. Lisa project aligns with my ongoing areas of research. My research has particularly focused on the role of the upper airways during these techniques, with the goal of enhancing respiratory care for patients, and I consider myself to be very suitable support for Lisa’s project. I am currently one of the world's leading physiotherapists in the field of chronic respiratory failure, including serving as the physiotherapy representative in a small expert group that organizes the multidisciplinary World Congress on Home Mechanical Ventilation (JIVD Congress), as well as the "Respiratory Failure and Mechanical Ventilation" course under the auspices of the world's largest respiratory organization, the European Respiratory Society (ERS). Through many years of work in an international environment, I have also developed a significant interdisciplinary network across several countries, where we actively collaborate on everything from clinical reasoning to drafting international guidelines. After my PhD defence in 2018, I took on my first roles as a co- supervisor for two PhD students who successfully defended their theses in 2022 and 2024. Currently, I lead my own research group with four active PhD students, for whom I serve as the primary supervisor. This includes three physiotherapists and one medical doctor specializing in pulmonology. Additionally, I have supervised 10 master's students who have successfully completed their degrees and am currently supervising two additional master's students. Further, I have engaged as co-supervisor for Lisa Edel in her project in the planning phase. Full Name Dr Ema Swingwood Organisation UNIVERSITY HOSPITALS BRISTOL AND WESTON NHS FOUNDATION TRUST Email Address Ema.Swingwood@uwe.ac.uk Role Practice Supervisor Description of Support Alongside my clinical consultant role, I am a Clinical Research Fellow at Provided the University of the West of England (UWE) and a visiting lecturer at UWE and University College London for undergraduate and post- graduate Physiotherapy programmes. I have 20 years of clinical experience specifically in respiratory (both adults and paediatrics) and have been extensively involved in cough augmentation and airway clearance using the mechanical insufflation- exsufflation device, so have an excellent understanding of the clinical area that Lisa wishes to explore. I have lectured at International conferences over a number of years around these topics. Having completed my MSc in 2012, I have continued my research with my PhD work, which explored the use of MI-E in the intubated population. I currently hold an NIHR SCPRA award 2025-2027. I am the research lead for Diagnostics and Therapies at UHBW where a key component of my role is embedding research into the clinical setting and developing a research infrastructure for non-medics. My experiences with this will enable me to work effectively with Lisa during and beyond her PhD to develop a clinical academic role. I have supervised 9 MSc students (University College London) to completion, am supervising numerous ICA Internship applications, and am named as a clinical academic supervisor in another doctoral application. I have known Lisa for approximately five years, initially in a clinical capacity and through links within key national respiratory stakeholder groups, and more recently through informal mentoring. Lisa is a national expert in her field of the respiratory management of neuromuscular paediatrics patients. She has sought out opportunities to initially develop her research capability and research network. She is dedicated to optimising the care of her patients and her own development and I have no doubt that she will excel in this fellowship. I am thrilled to be able to support her and look forward to working with Lisa on this fascinating programme. Full Name Professor Giovanni Baranello Organisation University College London Email Address g.baranello@ucl.ac.uk Role Primary Academic Supervisor Description of Support I have had the opportunity to work closely with Lisa over the past 6 Provided years as neuromuscular consultant at the Great Ormond Street Hospital and we have collaborated on several projects, like the correlation between a clinical respiratory score and a more objective respiratory assessment using the optoelectronic plethysmography. Lisa has a strong interest in developing research in the respiratory management of children with neuromuscular diseases and has brilliant ideas on how to demonstrate that the respiratory physiotherapy management of airway clearance can lead to improved respiratory status of children with Spinal Muscular Atrophy (SMA). Lisa has shown herself to be intellectually excellent, hardworking, well- organized, and has a keen and enquiring mind. She has excellent written and oral presentation skills and interacts absolutely well with colleagues. She has authored a few publications, she is actively involved in national and international research networks, like the International SMA Consortium, that include research centres from the UK, Italy and the US, and has presented her research projects at national and international conferences. I am committed to provide Lisa with all the necessary supervision during her PhD project, should she be successful in her application. I have the time and expertise to do so. As clinician academic I regularly provide supervision to postgraduate students. In the last 5 years I have supervised 5 PhD students, 2 as primary, and 2 have achieved completion. The mission of the UCL Great Ormond Street Institute of Child Health where I am based is to improve the health and well-being of children, and the adults they will become, through world-class research, education and public engagement. The UCL GOS ICH, together with its clinical partner Great Ormond Street Hospital for Children, forms the largest concentration of children’s health research outside North America. The Dubowitz Neuromuscular Centre is the largest paediatric neuromuscular centre in the UK, and has a strong research interest in the causes and treatment of muscular dystrophies and other neuromuscular disorders in children. Along with myself, Professor Francesco Muntoni is also an academic member and lead of The Dubowitz Neuromuscular Centre. There are also other research members of the team that can provide support for Lisa’s research project, including 3 research physiotherapists and 3 clinical research fellows. I recommend Lisa Edel unreservedly for this PhD application. She is outstanding in terms of her personal and intellectual attributes. I am sure that she will be an excellent PhD student and will progress to a successful clinical-academic career. Full Name Dr Colin Butler Organisation GREAT ORMOND STREET HOSPITAL FOR CHILDREN NHS FOUNDATION TRUST Email Address colin.butler@gosh.nhs.uk Role Academic Supervisor Description of Support I am delighted to write in support Lisa Edel and her application. The Provided research proposal has the potential to significantly advance the care of children with Spinal Muscular Atrophy (SMA), where the focus of the research will be on understanding upper airway dynamicsechniques can be applied to improve mechanical insufflation and exsufflation in these children to improve and how novel t respiratory health. I am an academic ENT surgeon at Great Ormond Street Hospital (GOSH)and a senior clinical lecturer and PI of the EpiCENTR group at the Institute of Child Health at UCL. I have known Lisa for 2 years, both clinically and from a research perspective. At GOSH, she is a specialist in treating children with SMA, and her academic focus will undoubtedly make her a future leader in her field. The research proposal has the potential for high impact in improving respiratory health, not only in children with SMA but also has application to other pathologies. Lisa is in a unique position at Great Ormond Street to investigate children with this severe airway disease and has an excellent research track record to date. In relation to this proposal, I am pleased to offer assistance in supervising the clinical application of the endoscopy techniques proposed in the work and will support Lisa with the analysis and research methodologies outlined. Lisa will have access to the Ear Nose and Throat (ENT) research group at ICH, where there are weekly research group meetings with airway-based research clinicians.. The Institue of Child Health will provide Lisa appropriate postdoctoral research support, with appropriate training in place. My research group at ICH focuses on paediatric ENT research (clinical and laboratory based), where we have an active group of PhD and MSc students. I currently co-supervise 2 PhD students and 2 MSc students. I have completed supervision on 1 PhD student (completed within 4 years) and 2 MD students where they were awarded their degrees respectively. I also currently co-supervise a postdoctoral NIHR clinical lecturer in paediatric ENT. If awarded, Lisa will have regular supervision and be able gain peer support from the group of doctoral students linked to our group. A benefit of the cross-disciplinary approach will mean she will have support from several allied specialities. As an ENT consultant specialising in airway research, I can offer clinical training to Lisa in the endoscopy techniques required for the proposal and provide supportive analysis of the proposed in application. Our group has experience in both quantitative and As an ENT consultant specialising in airway research, I can provide clinical training to Lisa in the endoscopy techniques required for the proposal and offer supportive analysis of the proposed application. Our group has experience in both quantitative and qualitative research and in developing validated outcomes to assess airway pathologies. In short, I am enthusiastic about supporting Lisa and this project, which I believe has the potential to enhance healthcare, not only in the targeted area but also for children with significant respiratory diseases in general. Full Name Ms Deborah Ridout Organisation UCL Great Ormond Street Institute of Child Health Email Address d.ridout@ucl.ac.uk Role Academic Supervisor Description of Support As Director of the statistical support service at the Institute of Child Provided Health, I have more than 20 years of experience collaborating with colleagues throughout the Institute, UCL and with external academic and industry partners. This has allowed me the opportunity to supervise and oversee many PhD and MSc students. In the last 6 years I have supervised 2 PhD students to completion and have been part of the supervisory panel for 6 others. One of my main areas of research is paediatric neuromuscular disease and I have collaborated extensively with experts in the field and have co-authored over 30 related publications. I have expertise in respiratory outcome measures in the patient population in the project and have supported many colleagues and students in understanding this type of data from a statistical perspective. I have helped design and set up many successful studies and am experienced in supporting and guiding students to develop their research skills. I am a statistics expert and can guide Lisa in her training and support her in applying her newly developed skills to her project. I have known Lisa for more than 6 years and found her to be a dedicated researcher who is keen and ambitious to explore new areas of research. I am committed to providing Lisa with all the necessary supervision during her PhD project, should she be successful in her application and have no doubt she will be an excellent student. Full Name Professor Dr Faith Gibson Organisation GREAT ORMOND STREET HOSPITAL FOR CHILDREN NHS FOUNDATION TRUST Email Address faith.gibson@gosh.nhs.uk Role Academic Supervisor Description of Support Lisa and I have worked together in terms of clinical academic activities Provided across the trust over the last 5 years. I am aware of Lisa’s profile and activities in research. I feel she is ready for a fellowship like this one and she is ready to move along in her chosen career of academic clinical research. She is ready to lead more research and support others in their research development. There is a lot of support for Lisa. She joins a thriving academic community. ORCHID is a research centre; Lisa is part of this centre and therefore benefits through interactions with PhD prepared nurses and Allied Health Professionals: in the clinical academic faculty we currently support 16 DCAF students, 5 PCAFs, 10 interns, and 13 post-docs, 1ACAF. We have a regular programme of activities for students, bringing them together with the Research Faculty: researchers on time- limited studies, that includes social scientists and health service researchers. We receive some support from the GOSH BRC and have an established vibrant internship programme and have huge success with the various NIHR programmes. My role as Director of this Centre is to actively support nurses and allied health professionals on their academic pathway, whoever is funding their fellowship, as well as supporting and mentoring other researchers within the Centre. To date I have been part of16 supervisory teams, I was primary supervisor for 7 of these: all successful and timely completions. I currently have 3 PhD students. I have undertaken 28 PhD vivas. My research expertise is qualitative and uses creative approaches to work with young children. My main field of research is children and young people who have cancer. I am a children’s cancer nurse with over 30 years’ experience in this speciality. My specific role is to support Lisa with her study design and working to develop questions to be used with families in finding out the tolerance, acceptability and comfort when receiving the intervention. Where there are no validated scales available, we have worked with PPI and communities, that includes healthcare professionals to devise suitable questions that help us to understand patient and family experiences. Families with lived experience in particular can help us to find the right questions, the right way to ask and when to ask. We have published some of this work, see for example: Kate Oulton, Naomi Oldrieve, Julie Bayliss, Victoria Jones, Isobel Manning, Lisa Shipway & Faith Gibson (2017): Using participatory and creative research methods to develop and pilot an informative game for preparing children for blood tests, Arts & Health, DOI:10.1080/17533015.2017.1392329

criteria.name: Common Characteristics of Good Applications
criteria.definition: The application demonstrates characteristics commonly found in strong grant applications.

signals.signal_text: Clear research vision and career trajectory
Distinctive ideas with an independent direction
Well-justified training plan
Strong mentorship and institutional support

criteria.pros: The application demonstrates a clear research vision and career trajectory (g.1.a) with supervisors explicitly outlining the path from clinical practice to academic leadership. Strong mentorship (g.1.d) is evidenced by a multi-disciplinary team with specific roles and extensive prior supervision experience. The training plan (g.1.c) is well-justified with specific courses linked to project needs. Distinctive ideas (g.1.b) are present, focusing on airway dynamics in SMA, though the 'independent direction' is heavily supported by the supervisor's existing methods.
criteria.drawbacks: While the vision is clear, the 'independent direction' (g.1.b) relies significantly on the supervisor's established methodology (continuous laryngoscopy during MI-E), suggesting the applicant is extending existing work rather than pioneering a wholly new independent direction. The training plan is strong but could be more explicit on how it fills specific gaps beyond the project scope.
criteria.rationale: While the vision is clear, the 'independent direction' (g.1.b) relies significantly on the supervisor's established methodology (continuous laryngoscopy during MI-E), suggesting the applicant is extending existing work rather than pioneering a wholly new independent direction. The training plan is strong but could be more explicit on how it fills specific gaps beyond the project scope.

evidence.text: e and back up of all project data. Cost is based on 2TB (£390) external hard drive and a 128GB memory stick (£70). A laptop (£1000) has also been costed in the equipment section. Consumables The costs include: - Cleaning of the Transnasal fibreoptic Laryngoscopes ( £38 per scope for 50 patients = £1900) - Double sided stickers for opto-electronic plethysmography: Single use stickers for the OEP, 52 stickers required per patients per visit = 2,600 stickers required (£60 per 500) - Printing costs for posters for conference - Printing costs for study related materials Knowledge mobilisation, dissemination and impact Costings include production of podcasts and talking heads production, to ensure equitable dissemination of information. Training and development Courses included in the career development plan are a combination of free and paid for courses. PPIE workshops and the statistics course are free to GOSH/UCL staff. This section accounts for the PhD tuition fees of £23048.40 for 3 years. Paid for courses include for a total cost of £4682.40; - The Kings Fund leadership course: The programme will equip leaders with the skills needed to manage a team, build resilience and respond to

an ever-changing external environment. - Bronchoscopy course at The Royal Brompton Hospital: A 2 day course for learning use of bronchoscopies (School of Bronchoscopy | Royal Brompton & Harefield hospitals) - Beyond BASIC Airway Management Course at The Royal Brompton Hospital: To increase skills with management of the upper airway (Beyond BASIC Airway Management Course | Royal Brompton & Harefield hospitals) - Impact Acceleration Unit, Knowledge mobilisation at Keele University: This is a fantastic opportunity for clinicians, academics and members of the public to improve their knowledge and understanding of the practical ways to design and use knowledge mobilisation theories and strategies in health and care practice. Research inclusion Courses related to research inclusion are included in the PhD programme and are free of charge. Access to talking mats to allow for all children to participate in consent and feedback. The annual fee is £120 for the complete package (https://www.talkingmats.com/new-digital-app-subscriptions/) Working with people and communities The costs relate to time, travel and subsistence for the committees involved in the project. criteria.name: Tell Us Why You Need This Award
criteria.definition: The applicant clearly articulates why this specific award is the right next step in their career.
criteria.group_name: Tell Us Why You Need This Award

signals.signal_text: Explain why this award is the right next step in your career (not just another project grant)
What difference would securing an award make to your career, and why now?
How will an award build on your existing experience and what new skills will it enable you to gain?
How will it develop your research partnerships within the NHS, public health or social care, charity organisations, industry or other organisations?

criteria.pros: The application articulates why this award is the right next step (g.2.a) by positioning the applicant as a national expert ready to lead research. The award will build on existing experience (g.2.c) by enabling the acquisition of specific skills like bronchoscopy and advanced airway management. Partnerships (g.2.d) are well-developed, with links to international networks and charity partners. The difference the award makes (g.2.b) is implied through the transition to a clinical-academic role.
criteria.drawbacks: The explicit statement of 'why now' (g.2.b) is somewhat implicit in the supervisors' comments about readiness rather than a direct statement from the applicant. The specific difference the award makes to the career trajectory is described more in terms of skill acquisition than a transformative career shift, though the move to a clinical-academic role is clear.
criteria.rationale: The explicit statement of 'why now' (g.2.b) is somewhat implicit in the supervisors' comments about readiness rather than a direct statement from the applicant. The specific difference the award makes to the career trajectory is described more in terms of skill acquisition than a transformative career shift, though the move to a clinical-academic role is clear.

SUMMARY INFORMATION > Application Title:
Can imaging of airway dynamics optimise assisted coughing in children with spinal muscular atrophy?

GREAT ORMOND STREET HOSPITAL FOR CHILDREN NHS FOUNDATION TRUST

LEAD APPLICANT & RESEARCH TEAM > Lead Applicant > Full Name:
Mrs Lisa Edel

LEAD APPLICANT & RESEARCH TEAM > Lead Applicant > Organisation:
GREAT ORMOND STREET HOSPITAL FOR CHILDREN NHS FOUNDATION TRUST

LEAD APPLICANT & RESEARCH TEAM > Lead Applicant > Department:
Physiotherapy Professional Background Allied Health Professional Detailed Professional Physiotherapist Background Professional Registration HCPC: PH88636

LEAD APPLICANT & RESEARCH TEAM > Lead Applicant > ORCID:
0000-0002-9352-5388

SUPPORT AND MENTORSHIP:
Organisation Western Norway University of Applied Sciences Email Address tiina.maarit.andersen@helse-bergen.no Role Practice Supervisor Description of Support I have know Lisa since 2021 through her impressive contributions to the Provided field of paediatric respiratory physiotherapy, particularly her work with children affected by neuromuscular conditions. Her innovative approach to clinical practice and research in this niche area has gained international recognition and she has significantly contributed to improving patient care. Lisa has a robust research foundation prior to her PhD work, having participated in large-scale clinical trials and the development of novel respiratory outcome measures that are widely used internationally. Her dedication to translational research is evidenced by the implementation of her respiratory assessment tool in clinical settings worldwide, particularly for children with Spinal Muscular Atrophy. Her academic achievements, such as receiving the Lea Rose Award, further underscore her research capabilities. Additionally, her extensive international collaborations reflect her commitment to advancing standards of care and her ability to engage effectively with a global research community. Lisa has several supervisors, where most are located in the UK, where Lisa is collecting the data for the PhD. She has access to the patient population described in the project plan, and the equipment to perform the examinations. Additionally, I will contribute for Lisa to visit us and learn the method of transnasal fibreoptic laryngoscopy during MI-E manoeuvres from the research group that is currently world leader on this. I will support Lisa with the method of continuous laryngoscopy during the MI-E interventions, how to plan the examinations, perform them, and how to interpret the data, which is a method I developed in 2010. I developed the idea to use laryngoscopy to study the upper airways during dynamic physiotherapy treatment with a cough assist device. My PhD involved assessing the feasibility of the examination and determining normal findings in healthy individuals. Subsequently, I examined all Amyotrophic Lateral Sclerosis patients in our region and identified abnormalities in these complex cases. This was followed up to five years throughout disease progression, describing how these changes evolve over time. This research has significantly improved the clinical treatment of the most vulnerable and challenging patients with chronic respiratory failure. Following my PhD (2018), I continued working as a postdoctoral researcher with a 50% grant in 5 years from Western Norway Health Authorities, focusing on enhancing clinical respiratory support treatment for ALS patients. This multicentre study includes participants from Bergen, Norway, and Porto, Portugal. Additionally, we are investigating the role of the larynx in another motor neuron disease affecting both children and adults. Lisa project aligns with my ongoing areas of research. My research has particularly focused on the role of the upper airways during these techniques, with the goal of enhancing respiratory care for patients, and I consider myself to be very suitable support for Lisa’s project. I am currently one of the world's leading physiotherapists in the field of chronic respiratory failure, including serving as the physiotherapy representative in a small expert group that organizes the multidisciplinary World Congress on Home Mechanical Ventilation (JIVD Congress), as well as the "Respiratory Failure and Mechanical Ventilation" course under the auspices of the world's largest respiratory organization, the European Respiratory Society (ERS). Through many years of work in an international environment, I have also developed a significant interdisciplinary network across several countries, where we actively collaborate on everything from clinical reasoning to drafting international guidelines. After my PhD defence in 2018, I took on my first roles as a co- supervisor for two PhD students who successfully defended their theses in 2022 and 2024. Currently, I lead my own research group with four active PhD students, for whom I serve as the primary supervisor. This includes three physiotherapists and one medical doctor specializing in pulmonology. Additionally, I have supervised 10 master's students who have successfully completed their degrees and am currently supervising two additional master's students. Further, I have engaged as co-supervisor for Lisa Edel in her project in the planning phase. Full Name Dr Ema Swingwood Organisation UNIVERSITY HOSPITALS BRISTOL AND WESTON NHS FOUNDATION TRUST Email Address Ema.Swingwood@uwe.ac.uk Role Practice Supervisor Description of Support Alongside my clinical consultant role, I am a Clinical Research Fellow at Provided the University of the West of England (UWE) and a visiting lecturer at UWE and University College London for undergraduate and post- graduate Physiotherapy programmes. I have 20 years of clinical experience specifically in respiratory (both adults and paediatrics) and have been extensively involved in cough augmentation and airway clearance using the mechanical insufflation- exsufflation device, so have an excellent understanding of the clinical area that Lisa wishes to explore. I have lectured at International conferences over a number of years around these topics. Having completed my MSc in 2012, I have continued my research with my PhD work, which explored the use of MI-E in the intubated population. I currently hold an NIHR SCPRA award 2025-2027. I am the research lead for Diagnostics and Therapies at UHBW where a key component of my role is embedding research into the clinical setting and developing a research infrastructure for non-medics. My experiences with this will enable me to work effectively with Lisa during and beyond her PhD to develop a clinical academic role. I have supervised 9 MSc students (University College London) to completion, am supervising numerous ICA Internship applications, and am named as a clinical academic supervisor in another doctoral application. I have known Lisa for approximately five years, initially in a clinical capacity and through links within key national respiratory stakeholder groups, and more recently through informal mentoring. Lisa is a national expert in her field of the respiratory management of neuromuscular paediatrics patients. She has sought out opportunities to initially develop her research capability and research network. She is dedicated to optimising the care of her patients and her own development and I have no doubt that she will excel in this fellowship. I am thrilled to be able to support her and look forward to working with Lisa on this fascinating programme. Full Name Professor Giovanni Baranello Organisation University College London Email Address g.baranello@ucl.ac.uk Role Primary Academic Supervisor Description of Support I have had the opportunity to work closely with Lisa over the past 6 Provided years as neuromuscular consultant at the Great Ormond Street Hospital and we have collaborated on several projects, like the correlation between a clinical respiratory score and a more objective respiratory assessment using the optoelectronic plethysmography. Lisa has a strong interest in developing research in the respiratory management of children with neuromuscular diseases and has brilliant ideas on how to demonstrate that the respiratory physiotherapy management of airway clearance can lead to improved respiratory status of children with Spinal Muscular Atrophy (SMA). Lisa has shown herself to be intellectually excellent, hardworking, well- organized, and has a keen and enquiring mind. She has excellent written and oral presentation skills and interacts absolutely well with colleagues. She has authored a few publications, she is actively involved in national and international research networks, like the International SMA Consortium, that include research centres from the UK, Italy and the US, and has presented her research projects at national and international conferences. I am committed to provide Lisa with all the necessary supervision during her PhD project, should she be successful in her application. I have the time and expertise to do so. As clinician academic I regularly provide supervision to postgraduate students. In the last 5 years I have supervised 5 PhD students, 2 as primary, and 2 have achieved completion. The mission of the UCL Great Ormond Street Institute of Child Health where I am based is to improve the health and well-being of children, and the adults they will become, through world-class research, education and public engagement. The UCL GOS ICH, together with its clinical partner Great Ormond Street Hospital for Children, forms the largest concentration of children’s health research outside North America. The Dubowitz Neuromuscular Centre is the largest paediatric neuromuscular centre in the UK, and has a strong research interest in the causes and treatment of muscular dystrophies and other neuromuscular disorders in children. Along with myself, Professor Francesco Muntoni is also an academic member and lead of The Dubowitz Neuromuscular Centre. There are also other research members of the team that can provide support for Lisa’s research project, including 3 research physiotherapists and 3 clinical research fellows. I recommend Lisa Edel unreservedly for this PhD application. She is outstanding in terms of her personal and intellectual attributes. I am sure that she will be an excellent PhD student and will progress to a successful clinical-academic career. Full Name Dr Colin Butler Organisation GREAT ORMOND STREET HOSPITAL FOR CHILDREN NHS FOUNDATION TRUST Email Address colin.butler@gosh.nhs.uk Role Academic Supervisor Description of Support I am delighted to write in support Lisa Edel and her application. The Provided research proposal has the potential to significantly advance the care of children with Spinal Muscular Atrophy (SMA), where the focus of the research will be on understanding upper airway dynamicsechniques can be applied to improve mechanical insufflation and exsufflation in these children to improve and how novel t respiratory health. I am an academic ENT surgeon at Great Ormond Street Hospital (GOSH)and a senior clinical lecturer and PI of the EpiCENTR group at the Institute of Child Health at UCL. I have known Lisa for 2 years, both clinically and from a research perspective. At GOSH, she is a specialist in treating children with SMA, and her academic focus will undoubtedly make her a future leader in her field. The research proposal has the potential for high impact in improving respiratory health, not only in children with SMA but also has application to other pathologies. Lisa is in a unique position at Great Ormond Street to investigate children with this severe airway disease and has an excellent research track record to date. In relation to this proposal, I am pleased to offer assistance in supervising the clinical application of the endoscopy techniques proposed in the work and will support Lisa with the analysis and research methodologies outlined. Lisa will have access to the Ear Nose and Throat (ENT) research group at ICH, where there are weekly research group meetings with airway-based research clinicians.. The Institue of Child Health will provide Lisa appropriate postdoctoral research support, with appropriate training in place. My research group at ICH focuses on paediatric ENT research (clinical and laboratory based), where we have an active group of PhD and MSc students. I currently co-supervise 2 PhD students and 2 MSc students. I have completed supervision on 1 PhD student (completed within 4 years) and 2 MD students where they were awarded their degrees respectively. I also currently co-supervise a postdoctoral NIHR clinical lecturer in paediatric ENT. If awarded, Lisa will have regular supervision and be able gain peer support from the group of doctoral students linked to our group. A benefit of the cross-disciplinary approach will mean she will have support from several allied specialities. As an ENT consultant specialising in airway research, I can offer clinical training to Lisa in the endoscopy techniques required for the proposal and provide supportive analysis of the proposed in application. Our group has experience in both quantitative and As an ENT consultant specialising in airway research, I can provide clinical training to Lisa in the endoscopy techniques required for the proposal and offer supportive analysis of the proposed application. Our group has experience in both quantitative and qualitative research and in developing validated outcomes to assess airway pathologies. In short, I am enthusiastic about supporting Lisa and this project, which I believe has the potential to enhance healthcare, not only in the targeted area but also for children with significant respiratory diseases in general. Full Name Ms Deborah Ridout Organisation UCL Great Ormond Street Institute of Child Health Email Address d.ridout@ucl.ac.uk Role Academic Supervisor Description of Support As Director of the statistical support service at the Institute of Child Provided Health, I have more than 20 years of experience collaborating with colleagues throughout the Institute, UCL and with external academic and industry partners. This has allowed me the opportunity to supervise and oversee many PhD and MSc students. In the last 6 years I have supervised 2 PhD students to completion and have been part of the supervisory panel for 6 others. One of my main areas of research is paediatric neuromuscular disease and I have collaborated extensively with experts in the field and have co-authored over 30 related publications. I have expertise in respiratory outcome measures in the patient population in the project and have supported many colleagues and students in understanding this type of data from a statistical perspective. I have helped design and set up many successful studies and am experienced in supporting and guiding students to develop their research skills. I am a statistics expert and can guide Lisa in her training and support her in applying her newly developed skills to her project. I have known Lisa for more than 6 years and found her to be a dedicated researcher who is keen and ambitious to explore new areas of research. I am committed to providing Lisa with all the necessary supervision during her PhD project, should she be successful in her application and have no doubt she will be an excellent student. Full Name Professor Dr Faith Gibson Organisation GREAT ORMOND STREET HOSPITAL FOR CHILDREN NHS FOUNDATION TRUST Email Address faith.gibson@gosh.nhs.uk Role Academic Supervisor Description of Support Lisa and I have worked together in terms of clinical academic activities Provided across the trust over the last 5 years. I am aware of Lisa’s profile and activities in research. I feel she is ready for a fellowship like this one and she is ready to move along in her chosen career of academic clinical research. She is ready to lead more research and support others in their research development. There is a lot of support for Lisa. She joins a thriving academic community. ORCHID is a research centre; Lisa is part of this centre and therefore benefits through interactions with PhD prepared nurses and Allied Health Professionals: in the clinical academic faculty we currently support 16 DCAF students, 5 PCAFs, 10 interns, and 13 post-docs, 1ACAF. We have a regular programme of activities for students, bringing them together with the Research Faculty: researchers on time- limited studies, that includes social scientists and health service researchers. We receive some support from the GOSH BRC and have an established vibrant internship programme and have huge success with the various NIHR programmes. My role as Director of this Centre is to actively support nurses and allied health professionals on their academic pathway, whoever is funding their fellowship, as well as supporting and mentoring other researchers within the Centre. To date I have been part of16 supervisory teams, I was primary supervisor for 7 of these: all successful and timely completions. I currently have 3 PhD students. I have undertaken 28 PhD vivas. My research expertise is qualitative and uses creative approaches to work with young children. My main field of research is children and young people who have cancer. I am a children’s cancer nurse with over 30 years’ experience in this speciality. My specific role is to support Lisa with her study design and working to develop questions to be used with families in finding out the tolerance, acceptability and comfort when receiving the intervention. Where there are no validated scales available, we have worked with PPI and communities, that includes healthcare professionals to devise suitable questions that help us to understand patient and family experiences. Families with lived experience in particular can help us to find the right questions, the right way to ask and when to ask. We have published some of this work, see for example: Kate Oulton, Naomi Oldrieve, Julie Bayliss, Victoria Jones, Isobel Manning, Lisa Shipway & Faith Gibson (2017): Using participatory and creative research methods to develop and pilot an informative game for preparing children for blood tests, Arts & Health, DOI:10.1080/17533015.2017.1392329

criteria.name: Relevant Research Experience and Training
criteria.definition: Demonstrates prior research experience and/or training that prepares the applicant to successfully undertake the proposed award in applied health or social care research.

signals.signal_text: Mentions of previous projects, research methods, or techniques relevant to the proposed study
Evidence of formal training, courses, or certifications
Alignment between prior experience and skills required for the proposed project

criteria.pros: The applicant has extensive relevant experience (g.3.a) including large-scale clinical trials and developing respiratory outcome measures. Formal training (g.3.b) is evidenced by the planned PhD and specific courses (Bronchoscopy, Beyond BASIC). There is strong alignment (g.3.c) between prior experience in neuromuscular respiratory care and the proposed study on SMA airway dynamics.
criteria.drawbacks: While the training plan is detailed, the evidence of *prior* formal training specifically in the advanced techniques proposed (e.g., TFL) is limited to the planned training, though the supervisor's support mitigates this gap.
criteria.rationale: While the training plan is detailed, the evidence of *prior* formal training specifically in the advanced techniques proposed (e.g., TFL) is limited to the planned training, though the supervisor's support mitigates this gap.

One of my main areas of research is paediatric neuromuscular disease and I have collaborated extensively with experts in the field and have co-authored over 30 related publications. I have expertise in respiratory outcome measures in the patient population in the project and have supported many colleagues and students in understanding this type of data from a statistical perspective. I have helped design and set up many successful studies and am experienced in supporting and guiding students to develop their research skills. I am a statistics expert and can guide Lisa in her training and support her in applying her newly developed skills to her project. I have known Lisa for more than 6 years and found her to be a dedicated researcher who is keen and ambitious to explore new areas of research. I am committed to providing Lisa with all the necessary supervision during her PhD project, should she be successful in her application and have no doubt she will be an excellent student. Full Name Professor Dr Faith Gibson Organisation GREAT ORMOND STREET HOSPITAL FOR CHILDREN NHS FOUNDATION TRUST Email Address faith.gibson@gosh.nhs.uk Role Academic Supervisor Description of Support Lisa and I have worked together in terms of clinical academic activities Provided across the trust over the last 5 years. I am aware of Lisa’s profile and activities in research. I feel she is ready for a fellowship like this one and she is ready to move along in her chosen career of academic clinical research. She is ready to lead more research and support others in their research development. There is a lot of support for Lisa. She joins a thriving academic community. ORCHID is a research centre; Lisa is part of this centre and therefore benefits through interactions with PhD prepared nurses and Allied Health Professionals: in the clinical academic faculty we currently support 16 DCAF students, 5 PCAFs, 10 interns, and 13 post-docs, 1ACAF. We have a regular programme of activities for students, bringing them together with the Research Faculty: researchers on time- limited studies, that includes social scientists and health service researchers. We receive some support from the GOSH BRC and have an established vibrant internship programme and have huge success with the various NIHR programmes. My role as Director of this Centre is to actively support nurses and allied health professionals on their academic pathway, whoever is funding their fellowship, as well as supporting and mentoring other researchers within the Centre. To date I have been part of16 supervisory teams, I was primary supervisor for 7 of these: all successful and timely completions. I currently have 3 PhD students. I have undertaken 28 PhD vivas. My research expertise is qualitative and uses creative approaches to work with young children. My main field of research is children and young people who have cancer. I am a children’s cancer nurse with over 30 years’ experience in this speciality. My specific role is to support Lisa with her study design and working to develop questions to be used with families in finding out the tolerance, acceptability and comfort when receiving the intervention. Where there are no validated scales available, we have worked with PPI and communities, that includes healthcare professionals to devise suitable questions that help us to understand patient and family experiences. Families with lived experience in particular can help us to find the right questions, the right way to ask and when to ask. We have published some of this work, see for example: Kate Oulton, Naomi Oldrieve, Julie Bayliss, Victoria Jones, Isobel Manning, Lisa Shipway & Faith Gibson (2017): Using participatory and creative research methods to develop and pilot an informative game for preparing children for blood tests, Arts & Health, DOI:10.1080/17533015.2017.1392329

criteria.name: Impactful Research Outputs and Outcomes
criteria.definition: Evidence of meaningful research outputs or outcomes appropriate to the applicant’s career stage, demonstrating contribution to the field.

signals.signal_text: Number and quality of publications, reports, or presentations
Citation metrics or other indicators of impact (where mentioned)
Awards, grants, or recognitions linked to prior research
Description of practical or societal impact of previous work

criteria.pros: The applicant has authored publications and presented at international conferences (g.4.a). Recognition includes the Lea Rose Award (g.4.c). Practical impact (g.4.d) is demonstrated by the implementation of her respiratory assessment tool in clinical settings worldwide.
criteria.drawbacks: Citation metrics or specific indicators of impact (g.4.b) are not explicitly mentioned in the text, only the existence of publications and awards. The number of publications is described as 'a few' rather than a substantial list, which limits the score for quantity.
criteria.rationale: Citation metrics or specific indicators of impact (g.4.b) are not explicitly mentioned in the text, only the existence of publications and awards. The number of publications is described as 'a few' rather than a substantial list, which limits the score for quantity.

Can imaging of airway dynamics optimise assisted coughing in children with spinal muscular atrophy?

SUMMARY INFORMATION > Contracting Organisation:
GREAT ORMOND STREET HOSPITAL FOR CHILDREN NHS FOUNDATION TRUST
