LEAD APPLICANT & RESEARCH TEAM > Lead Applicant > Full Name:
Mrs Lisa Edel

LEAD APPLICANT & RESEARCH TEAM > Lead Applicant > Organisation:
GREAT ORMOND STREET HOSPITAL FOR CHILDREN NHS FOUNDATION TRUST

LEAD APPLICANT & RESEARCH TEAM > Lead Applicant > Department:
Physiotherapy Professional Background Allied Health Professional Detailed Professional Physiotherapist Background Professional Registration HCPC: PH88636

LEAD APPLICANT & RESEARCH TEAM > Lead Applicant > ORCID:
0000-0002-9352-5388

SUPPORT AND MENTORSHIP:
Organisation Western Norway University of Applied Sciences Email Address tiina.maarit.andersen@helse-bergen.no Role Practice Supervisor Description of Support I have know Lisa since 2021 through her impressive contributions to the Provided field of paediatric respiratory physiotherapy, particularly her work with children affected by neuromuscular conditions. Her innovative approach to clinical practice and research in this niche area has gained international recognition and she has significantly contributed to improving patient care. Lisa has a robust research foundation prior to her PhD work, having participated in large-scale clinical trials and the development of novel respiratory outcome measures that are widely used internationally. Her dedication to translational research is evidenced by the implementation of her respiratory assessment tool in clinical settings worldwide, particularly for children with Spinal Muscular Atrophy. Her academic achievements, such as receiving the Lea Rose Award, further underscore her research capabilities. Additionally, her extensive international collaborations reflect her commitment to advancing standards of care and her ability to engage effectively with a global research community. Lisa has several supervisors, where most are located in the UK, where Lisa is collecting the data for the PhD. She has access to the patient population described in the project plan, and the equipment to perform the examinations. Additionally, I will contribute for Lisa to visit us and learn the method of transnasal fibreoptic laryngoscopy during MI-E manoeuvres from the research group that is currently world leader on this. I will support Lisa with the method of continuous laryngoscopy during the MI-E interventions, how to plan the examinations, perform them, and how to interpret the data, which is a method I developed in 2010. I developed the idea to use laryngoscopy to study the upper airways during dynamic physiotherapy treatment with a cough assist device. My PhD involved assessing the feasibility of the examination and determining normal findings in healthy individuals. Subsequently, I examined all Amyotrophic Lateral Sclerosis patients in our region and identified abnormalities in these complex cases. This was followed up to five years throughout disease progression, describing how these changes evolve over time. This research has significantly improved the clinical treatment of the most vulnerable and challenging patients with chronic respiratory failure. Following my PhD (2018), I continued working as a postdoctoral researcher with a 50% grant in 5 years from Western Norway Health Authorities, focusing on enhancing clinical respiratory support treatment for ALS patients. This multicentre study includes participants from Bergen, Norway, and Porto, Portugal. Additionally, we are investigating the role of the larynx in another motor neuron disease affecting both children and adults. Lisa project aligns with my ongoing areas of research. My research has particularly focused on the role of the upper airways during these techniques, with the goal of enhancing respiratory care for patients, and I consider myself to be very suitable support for Lisa’s project. I am currently one of the world's leading physiotherapists in the field of chronic respiratory failure, including serving as the physiotherapy representative in a small expert group that organizes the multidisciplinary World Congress on Home Mechanical Ventilation (JIVD Congress), as well as the "Respiratory Failure and Mechanical Ventilation" course under the auspices of the world's largest respiratory organization, the European Respiratory Society (ERS). Through many years of work in an international environment, I have also developed a significant interdisciplinary network across several countries, where we actively collaborate on everything from clinical reasoning to drafting international guidelines. After my PhD defence in 2018, I took on my first roles as a co- supervisor for two PhD students who successfully defended their theses in 2022 and 2024. Currently, I lead my own research group with four active PhD students, for whom I serve as the primary supervisor. This includes three physiotherapists and one medical doctor specializing in pulmonology. Additionally, I have supervised 10 master's students who have successfully completed their degrees and am currently supervising two additional master's students. Further, I have engaged as co-supervisor for Lisa Edel in her project in the planning phase. Full Name Dr Ema Swingwood Organisation UNIVERSITY HOSPITALS BRISTOL AND WESTON NHS FOUNDATION TRUST Email Address Ema.Swingwood@uwe.ac.uk Role Practice Supervisor Description of Support Alongside my clinical consultant role, I am a Clinical Research Fellow at Provided the University of the West of England (UWE) and a visiting lecturer at UWE and University College London for undergraduate and post- graduate Physiotherapy programmes. I have 20 years of clinical experience specifically in respiratory (both adults and paediatrics) and have been extensively involved in cough augmentation and airway clearance using the mechanical insufflation- exsufflation device, so have an excellent understanding of the clinical area that Lisa wishes to explore. I have lectured at International conferences over a number of years around these topics. Having completed my MSc in 2012, I have continued my research with my PhD work, which explored the use of MI-E in the intubated population. I currently hold an NIHR SCPRA award 2025-2027. I am the research lead for Diagnostics and Therapies at UHBW where a key component of my role is embedding research into the clinical setting and developing a research infrastructure for non-medics. My experiences with this will enable me to work effectively with Lisa during and beyond her PhD to develop a clinical academic role. I have supervised 9 MSc students (University College London) to completion, am supervising numerous ICA Internship applications, and am named as a clinical academic supervisor in another doctoral application. I have known Lisa for approximately five years, initially in a clinical capacity and through links within key national respiratory stakeholder groups, and more recently through informal mentoring. Lisa is a national expert in her field of the respiratory management of neuromuscular paediatrics patients. She has sought out opportunities to initially develop her research capability and research network. She is dedicated to optimising the care of her patients and her own development and I have no doubt that she will excel in this fellowship. I am thrilled to be able to support her and look forward to working with Lisa on this fascinating programme. Full Name Professor Giovanni Baranello Organisation University College London Email Address g.baranello@ucl.ac.uk Role Primary Academic Supervisor Description of Support I have had the opportunity to work closely with Lisa over the past 6 Provided years as neuromuscular consultant at the Great Ormond Street Hospital and we have collaborated on several projects, like the correlation between a clinical respiratory score and a more objective respiratory assessment using the optoelectronic plethysmography. Lisa has a strong interest in developing research in the respiratory management of children with neuromuscular diseases and has brilliant ideas on how to demonstrate that the respiratory physiotherapy management of airway clearance can lead to improved respiratory status of children with Spinal Muscular Atrophy (SMA). Lisa has shown herself to be intellectually excellent, hardworking, well- organized, and has a keen and enquiring mind. She has excellent written and oral presentation skills and interacts absolutely well with colleagues. She has authored a few publications, she is actively involved in national and international research networks, like the International SMA Consortium, that include research centres from the UK, Italy and the US, and has presented her research projects at national and international conferences. I am committed to provide Lisa with all the necessary supervision during her PhD project, should she be successful in her application. I have the time and expertise to do so. As clinician academic I regularly provide supervision to postgraduate students. In the last 5 years I have supervised 5 PhD students, 2 as primary, and 2 have achieved completion. The mission of the UCL Great Ormond Street Institute of Child Health where I am based is to improve the health and well-being of children, and the adults they will become, through world-class research, education and public engagement. The UCL GOS ICH, together with its clinical partner Great Ormond Street Hospital for Children, forms the largest concentration of children’s health research outside North America. The Dubowitz Neuromuscular Centre is the largest paediatric neuromuscular centre in the UK, and has a strong research interest in the causes and treatment of muscular dystrophies and other neuromuscular disorders in children. Along with myself, Professor Francesco Muntoni is also an academic member and lead of The Dubowitz Neuromuscular Centre. There are also other research members of the team that can provide support for Lisa’s research project, including 3 research physiotherapists and 3 clinical research fellows. I recommend Lisa Edel unreservedly for this PhD application. She is outstanding in terms of her personal and intellectual attributes. I am sure that she will be an excellent PhD student and will progress to a successful clinical-academic career. Full Name Dr Colin Butler Organisation GREAT ORMOND STREET HOSPITAL FOR CHILDREN NHS FOUNDATION TRUST Email Address colin.butler@gosh.nhs.uk Role Academic Supervisor Description of Support I am delighted to write in support Lisa Edel and her application. The Provided research proposal has the potential to significantly advance the care of children with Spinal Muscular Atrophy (SMA), where the focus of the research will be on understanding upper airway dynamicsechniques can be applied to improve mechanical insufflation and exsufflation in these children to improve and how novel t respiratory health. I am an academic ENT surgeon at Great Ormond Street Hospital (GOSH)and a senior clinical lecturer and PI of the EpiCENTR group at the Institute of Child Health at UCL. I have known Lisa for 2 years, both clinically and from a research perspective. At GOSH, she is a specialist in treating children with SMA, and her academic focus will undoubtedly make her a future leader in her field. The research proposal has the potential for high impact in improving respiratory health, not only in children with SMA but also has application to other pathologies. Lisa is in a unique position at Great Ormond Street to investigate children with this severe airway disease and has an excellent research track record to date. In relation to this proposal, I am pleased to offer assistance in supervising the clinical application of the endoscopy techniques proposed in the work and will support Lisa with the analysis and research methodologies outlined. Lisa will have access to the Ear Nose and Throat (ENT) research group at ICH, where there are weekly research group meetings with airway-based research clinicians.. The Institue of Child Health will provide Lisa appropriate postdoctoral research support, with appropriate training in place. My research group at ICH focuses on paediatric ENT research (clinical and laboratory based), where we have an active group of PhD and MSc students. I currently co-supervise 2 PhD students and 2 MSc students. I have completed supervision on 1 PhD student (completed within 4 years) and 2 MD students where they were awarded their degrees respectively. I also currently co-supervise a postdoctoral NIHR clinical lecturer in paediatric ENT. If awarded, Lisa will have regular supervision and be able gain peer support from the group of doctoral students linked to our group. A benefit of the cross-disciplinary approach will mean she will have support from several allied specialities. As an ENT consultant specialising in airway research, I can offer clinical training to Lisa in the endoscopy techniques required for the proposal and provide supportive analysis of the proposed in application. Our group has experience in both quantitative and As an ENT consultant specialising in airway research, I can provide clinical training to Lisa in the endoscopy techniques required for the proposal and offer supportive analysis of the proposed application. Our group has experience in both quantitative and qualitative research and in developing validated outcomes to assess airway pathologies. In short, I am enthusiastic about supporting Lisa and this project, which I believe has the potential to enhance healthcare, not only in the targeted area but also for children with significant respiratory diseases in general. Full Name Ms Deborah Ridout Organisation UCL Great Ormond Street Institute of Child Health Email Address d.ridout@ucl.ac.uk Role Academic Supervisor Description of Support As Director of the statistical support service at the Institute of Child Provided Health, I have more than 20 years of experience collaborating with colleagues throughout the Institute, UCL and with external academic and industry partners. This has allowed me the opportunity to supervise and oversee many PhD and MSc students. In the last 6 years I have supervised 2 PhD students to completion and have been part of the supervisory panel for 6 others. One of my main areas of research is paediatric neuromuscular disease and I have collaborated extensively with experts in the field and have co-authored over 30 related publications. I have expertise in respiratory outcome measures in the patient population in the project and have supported many colleagues and students in understanding this type of data from a statistical perspective. I have helped design and set up many successful studies and am experienced in supporting and guiding students to develop their research skills. I am a statistics expert and can guide Lisa in her training and support her in applying her newly developed skills to her project. I have known Lisa for more than 6 years and found her to be a dedicated researcher who is keen and ambitious to explore new areas of research. I am committed to providing Lisa with all the necessary supervision during her PhD project, should she be successful in her application and have no doubt she will be an excellent student. criteria.name: Trajectory Towards Research Leadership
criteria.definition: Demonstrates potential and progression towards becoming a future leader in health or social care research.

signals.signal_text: Statements of career ambitions and long-term goals
Evidence of increasing responsibility in previous roles (e.g., leading projects, mentoring)
Participation in leadership or professional development programs
Recognition by peers or institutions suggesting leadership potential

criteria.pros: Supervisors explicitly state the applicant is ready to lead more research and support others (g.5.a, g.5.b). Recognition by peers (g.5.d) is strong, with descriptions of her as a 'national expert' and 'outstanding'. Participation in leadership programs (g.5.c) is evidenced by the planned Kings Fund leadership course.
criteria.drawbacks: While the potential for leadership is clear, the evidence of *current* leadership in previous roles (g.5.b) is described more as 'contributing' and 'participating' in networks rather than leading large-scale independent projects, though the trajectory is evident.
criteria.rationale: While the potential for leadership is clear, the evidence of *current* leadership in previous roles (g.5.b) is described more as 'contributing' and 'participating' in networks rather than leading large-scale independent projects, though the trajectory is evident.

SUPPORT AND MENTORSHIP:
Organisation Western Norway University of Applied Sciences Email Address tiina.maarit.andersen@helse-bergen.no Role Practice Supervisor Description of Support I have know Lisa since 2021 through her impressive contributions to the Provided field of paediatric respiratory physiotherapy, particularly her work with children affected by neuromuscular conditions. Her innovative approach to clinical practice and research in this niche area has gained international recognition and she has significantly contributed to improving patient care. Lisa has a robust research foundation prior to her PhD work, having participated in large-scale clinical trials and the development of novel respiratory outcome measures that are widely used internationally. Her dedication to translational research is evidenced by the implementation of her respiratory assessment tool in clinical settings worldwide, particularly for children with Spinal Muscular Atrophy. Her academic achievements, such as receiving the Lea Rose Award, further underscore her research capabilities. Additionally, her extensive international collaborations reflect her commitment to advancing standards of care and her ability to engage effectively with a global research community. Lisa has several supervisors, where most are located in the UK, where Lisa is collecting the data for the PhD. She has access to the patient population described in the project plan, and the equipment to perform the examinations. Additionally, I will contribute for Lisa to visit us and learn the method of transnasal fibreoptic laryngoscopy during MI-E manoeuvres from the research group that is currently world leader on this. I will support Lisa with the method of continuous laryngoscopy during the MI-E interventions, how to plan the examinations, perform them, and how to interpret the data, which is a method I developed in 2010. I developed the idea to use laryngoscopy to study the upper airways during dynamic physiotherapy treatment with a cough assist device. My PhD involved assessing the feasibility of the examination and determining normal findings in healthy individuals. Subsequently, I examined all Amyotrophic Lateral Sclerosis patients in our region and identified abnormalities in these complex cases. This was followed up to five years throughout disease progression, describing how these changes evolve over time. This research has significantly improved the clinical treatment of the most vulnerable and challenging patients with chronic respiratory failure. Following my PhD (2018), I continued working as a postdoctoral researcher with a 50% grant in 5 years from Western Norway Health Authorities, focusing on enhancing clinical respiratory support treatment for ALS patients. This multicentre study includes participants from Bergen, Norway, and Porto, Portugal. Additionally, we are investigating the role of the larynx in another motor neuron disease affecting both children and adults. Lisa project aligns with my ongoing areas of research. My research has particularly focused on the role of the upper airways during these techniques, with the goal of enhancing respiratory care for patients, and I consider myself to be very suitable support for Lisa’s project. I am currently one of the world's leading physiotherapists in the field of chronic respiratory failure, including serving as the physiotherapy representative in a small expert group that organizes the multidisciplinary World Congress on Home Mechanical Ventilation (JIVD Congress), as well as the "Respiratory Failure and Mechanical Ventilation" course under the auspices of the world's largest respiratory organization, the European Respiratory Society (ERS). Through many years of work in an international environment, I have also developed a significant interdisciplinary network across several countries, where we actively collaborate on everything from clinical reasoning to drafting international guidelines. After my PhD defence in 2018, I took on my first roles as a co- supervisor for two PhD students who successfully defended their theses in 2022 and 2024. Currently, I lead my own research group with four active PhD students, for whom I serve as the primary supervisor. This includes three physiotherapists and one medical doctor specializing in pulmonology. Additionally, I have supervised 10 master's students who have successfully completed their degrees and am currently supervising two additional master's students. Further, I have engaged as co-supervisor for Lisa Edel in her project in the planning phase. Full Name Dr Ema Swingwood Organisation UNIVERSITY HOSPITALS BRISTOL AND WESTON NHS FOUNDATION TRUST Email Address Ema.Swingwood@uwe.ac.uk Role Practice Supervisor Description of Support Alongside my clinical consultant role, I am a Clinical Research Fellow at Provided the University of the West of England (UWE) and a visiting lecturer at UWE and University College London for undergraduate and post- graduate Physiotherapy programmes. I have 20 years of clinical experience specifically in respiratory (both adults and paediatrics) and have been extensively involved in cough augmentation and airway clearance using the mechanical insufflation- exsufflation device, so have an excellent understanding of the clinical area that Lisa wishes to explore. I have lectured at International conferences over a number of years around these topics. Having completed my MSc in 2012, I have continued my research with my PhD work, which explored the use of MI-E in the intubated population. I currently hold an NIHR SCPRA award 2025-2027. I am the research lead for Diagnostics and Therapies at UHBW where a key component of my role is embedding research into the clinical setting and developing a research infrastructure for non-medics. My experiences with this will enable me to work effectively with Lisa during and beyond her PhD to develop a clinical academic role. I have supervised 9 MSc students (University College London) to completion, am supervising numerous ICA Internship applications, and am named as a clinical academic supervisor in another doctoral application. I have known Lisa for approximately five years, initially in a clinical capacity and through links within key national respiratory stakeholder groups, and more recently through informal mentoring. Lisa is a national expert in her field of the respiratory management of neuromuscular paediatrics patients. She has sought out opportunities to initially develop her research capability and research network. She is dedicated to optimising the care of her patients and her own development and I have no doubt that she will excel in this fellowship. I am thrilled to be able to support her and look forward to working with Lisa on this fascinating programme. Full Name Professor Giovanni Baranello Organisation University College London Email Address g.baranello@ucl.ac.uk Role Primary Academic Supervisor Description of Support I have had the opportunity to work closely with Lisa over the past 6 Provided years as neuromuscular consultant at the Great Ormond Street Hospital and we have collaborated on several projects, like the correlation between a clinical respiratory score and a more objective respiratory assessment using the optoelectronic plethysmography. Lisa has a strong interest in developing research in the respiratory management of children with neuromuscular diseases and has brilliant ideas on how to demonstrate that the respiratory physiotherapy management of airway clearance can lead to improved respiratory status of children with Spinal Muscular Atrophy (SMA). Lisa has shown herself to be intellectually excellent, hardworking, well- organized, and has a keen and enquiring mind. She has excellent written and oral presentation skills and interacts absolutely well with colleagues. She has authored a few publications, she is actively involved in national and international research networks, like the International SMA Consortium, that include research centres from the UK, Italy and the US, and has presented her research projects at national and international conferences. I am committed to provide Lisa with all the necessary supervision during her PhD project, should she be successful in her application. I have the time and expertise to do so. As clinician academic I regularly provide supervision to postgraduate students. In the last 5 years I have supervised 5 PhD students, 2 as primary, and 2 have achieved completion. The mission of the UCL Great Ormond Street Institute of Child Health where I am based is to improve the health and well-being of children, and the adults they will become, through world-class research, education and public engagement. The UCL GOS ICH, together with its clinical partner Great Ormond Street Hospital for Children, forms the largest concentration of children’s health research outside North America. The Dubowitz Neuromuscular Centre is the largest paediatric neuromuscular centre in the UK, and has a strong research interest in the causes and treatment of muscular dystrophies and other neuromuscular disorders in children. Along with myself, Professor Francesco Muntoni is also an academic member and lead of The Dubowitz Neuromuscular Centre. There are also other research members of the team that can provide support for Lisa’s research project, including 3 research physiotherapists and 3 clinical research fellows. I recommend Lisa Edel unreservedly for this PhD application. She is outstanding in terms of her personal and intellectual attributes. I am sure that she will be an excellent PhD student and will progress to a successful clinical-academic career. Full Name Dr Colin Butler Organisation GREAT ORMOND STREET HOSPITAL FOR CHILDREN NHS FOUNDATION TRUST Email Address colin.butler@gosh.nhs.uk Role Academic Supervisor Description of Support I am delighted to write in support Lisa Edel and her application. At GOSH, she is a specialist in treating children with SMA, and her academic focus will undoubtedly make her a future leader in her field. sub_criteria.pros: The Plain English Summary is exceptionally clear, with NLP metrics confirming high readability (Flesch-Kincaid 4.79) and low jargon density (1.13%). It comprehensively covers the problem (SMA/mucus), objectives, methods (camera, stickers), beneficiaries, and impact (reduced hospital visits). Technical terms like 'cough-assist' are explained simply, and the content aligns perfectly with the Scientific Abstract.
sub_criteria.drawbacks: No material caveats or missing details found; the summary is a model of accessibility and alignment.

evidence.text:
Spinal Muscular Atrophy (SMA) is a condition which affects how long you can live, mostly affecting
children. SMA weakens muscles, including those needed to move limbs, swallow, breathe and cough.
As a result of weakened breathing muscles, a thick and sticky substance known as mucus builds up in
the child’s lungs, causing difficulty breathing. This leads to infections in their lungs and repeated hospital
visits affecting children’s quality of life and life expectancy.
Physiotherapists work with these children and their parents to prevent them from becoming unwell. One
way these children can be helped is by daily use of a machine referred to as a cough-assist. This mimics
a cough clearing mucus from the lungs. A child needs to wear a mask for a short time over their nose
and mouth as air is blown into their lungs. This helps them take a big breath in, followed by creating a
vacuum to move mucus out.
In SMA, we need to understand what happens to the child’s airway (breathing tube) whilst breathing,
during coughing and when using cough-assist.
We need more knowledge about how cough-assist settings help improve mucus clearance, reducing the
risk of chest infections, improving quality of
evidence.source_path: APPLICATION DETAILS > Plain English Summary of Research

evidence.text:
life for patients. This could also reduce hospital visits and
costs to the NHS.
Research aims and design:
This will be based at Great Ormond Street Hospital.
We need to do the research in three parts.
Part 1. Using a small (2mm) camera through the nose, we will view and assess what happens to the
airway of children with SMA when:
a. They breathe on their own and use their home cough-assist
b. We change cough-assist settings to see if this improves coughing.
These tests will not hurt. We will record what the camera shows us.
Part 2. Measuring the breathing patterns of children when relaxed and when using cough-assist.
The child taking part will lie down on a bed and relax, breathing normally. Between 24-52 special
stickers will be put on their chest. The number of markers depends on the size of the child. Eight
cameras in the ceiling will pick up signals from these stickers and show us the child’s breathing pattern.
We will be able to measure the amount of air in the child’s lungs, breath by breath. We will measure
breathing at rest, breaths with the original cough-assist settings as well as breaths with adjusted cough-
assist settings from part 1. We will then measure the differen
evidence.source_path: APPLICATION DETAILS > Plain English Summary of Research

evidence.text:
ces in breathing patterns. This will highlight
any changes in the way children with SMA breath, having potential benefit in clearing more mucus out
from the lungs.
Part 3. Understand child's', parents' and carers' views, tolerance, and acceptance of the new
assessment techniques.
During the tests, children will be asked to rate the new tests in terms of acceptance, comfort, and
tolerance on a visual scale.
Sharing results
We will include children and their parents during the study. They will guide us in the best ways to share
information about what we find. We will also share what we find at meetings and conferences (In the
United Kingdom and internationally). We will write about what we learnt and share this through medical
and physiotherapy journals.

sub_criteria.pros: The application clearly identifies a specific knowledge gap: the lack of understanding regarding airway dynamics during MI-E use in children with SMA. This gap is explicitly stated in both the Scientific Abstract and Plain English Summary, justifying the need for the study.

evidence.text:
Research Question
Can imaging of airway dynamics optimise assisted coughing in children with spinal muscular atrophy?
Background
Spinal Muscular Atrophy(SMA) is a life-limiting condition affecting children’s ability to move limbs,
swallow, breathe and cough effectively, impacting quality of life and life expectancy. Patients develop
increased secretions, causing recurrent chest infections and hospital admissions. Respiratory
physiotherapy relies on Mechanical Insufflation Exsufflation (MI-E) devices to help prevent infections.
MI-E delivers positive pressure promoting deep inhalation followed by a rapid switch to negative
pressure to mimic a cough. MI-E are programmed on specific settings for children to use at home.
However, there is limited understanding of what occurs in the child’s airway when breathing, coughing,
and using MI-E. Concerns include potential airway collapse and MI-E effectiveness. Understanding
upper airway response to using MI-E is vital to improve patient outcomes
Aims
Investigate reasons for variability in effectiveness of MI-E in children, establishing optimal settings for
MI-E in patients with SMA
Assess variations in lung function with optimised MI-E settin

visits affecting children’s quality of life and life expectancy.

and mouth as air is blown into their lungs. This helps them take a big breath in, followed by creating a

gs
Evaluate tolerance and acceptance of new assessment techniques
Objectives
Work Package(WP)1a: Assess the upper airway dynamics using Transnasal Fibreoptic
Laryngoscope(TFL) in children with SMA
WP1b: Evaluate/optimise upper airway response patterns to MI-E
WP2: Assess changes in respiratory patterns/lung volumes utilising opto-electronic
plethysmography(OEP) with baseline and optimised MI-E settings
WP3: Measure patients'/carers' tolerance and acceptability of assessment techniques
Method
An interventional quantitative study consisting of three WPs
45 patients will be recruited. In WP1 Patients will be assessed with TFL during normal breathing and use
of home MI-E settings. Airway status will then be analysed with MI-E being adjusted for optimal airway
status. In WP2, OEP will measure differences in breathing patterns and lung volumes, before MI-E, at
baseline MI-E settings and on adjusted MI-E settings. During WP1 and WP2, patients/carers will rate
tests in terms of tolerance and acceptance on a visual scale (WP3). Statistical analysis will be
conducted. Data synthesis will confirm challenges, strengths, and areas for improvement.
Timelines for delivery
WP1, 2 and 3: 9-18Months

gs
Evaluate tolerance and acceptance of new assessment techniques
Objectives
Work Package(WP)1a: Assess the upper airway dynamics using Transnasal Fibreoptic
Laryngoscope(TFL) in children with SMA
WP1b: Evaluate/optimise upper airway response patterns to MI-E
WP2: Assess changes in respiratory patterns/lung volumes utilising opto-electronic
plethysmography(OEP) with baseline and optimised MI-E settings
WP3: Measure patients'/carers' tolerance and acceptability of assessment techniques
Method
An interventional quantitative study consisting of three WPs
45 patients will be recruited. In WP1 Patients will be assessed with TFL during normal breathing and use
of home MI-E settings. Airway status will then be analysed with MI-E being adjusted for optimal airway
status. In WP2, OEP will measure differences in breathing patterns and lung volumes, before MI-E, at
baseline MI-E settings and on adjusted MI-E settings. During WP1 and WP2, patients/carers will rate
tests in terms of tolerance and acceptance on a visual scale (WP3). Statistical analysis will be
conducted. Data synthesis will confirm challenges, strengths, and areas for improvement.
Timelines for delivery
WP1, 2 and 3: 9-18Months

We will include children and their parents during the study. They will guide us in the best ways to share

evidence.text:
Knowledge mobilisation, dissemination, and impact
Improved secretion management will reduce hospitalisations improving health and wellbeing. The study
provides insight into airway dynamics and will translate into other paediatric conditions. Results will be
shared via podcasts and meetings with parent support groups/charities, and presentations at
meetings/conferences, throughout the UK and internationally, including submission to appropriate peer
reviewed journals.
Research Inclusion
The study focuses on paediatric patients with SMA, which as a rare disease serves an under-
represented population from all cultural backgrounds. It is supported by community steering groups.
Adjustments have been made to ensure patients with behavioural and communicative concerns are able
to participate. Patient information sheets will be available to all and adapted for different communication
styles. An animated video has been created to aid inclusion. Characteristics related to inclusion of
participants will be reported. Ongoing reviews will ensure equality, diversity and inclusion are
considered.

Spinal Muscular Atrophy (SMA) is a condition which affects how long you can live, mostly affecting
children. SMA weakens muscles, including those needed to move limbs, swallow, breathe and cough.
As a result of weakened breathing muscles, a thick and sticky substance known as mucus builds up in
the child’s lungs, causing difficulty breathing. This leads to infections in their lungs and repeated hospital
visits affecting children’s quality of life and life expectancy.
Physiotherapists work with these children and their parents to prevent them from becoming unwell. One
way these children can be helped is by daily use of a machine referred to as a cough-assist. This mimics
a cough clearing mucus from the lungs. A child needs to wear a mask for a short time over their nose
and mouth as air is blown into their lungs. This helps them take a big breath in, followed by creating a
vacuum to move mucus out.
In SMA, we need to understand what happens to the child’s airway (breathing tube) whilst breathing,
during coughing and when using cough-assist.
We need more knowledge about how cough-assist settings help improve mucus clearance, reducing the
risk of chest infections, improving quality of

evidence.text: Travel and Subsistence The budget focuses on two conferences, in alignment with the finance guidance. The costings relate to World Muscle Society conference and Cure SMA. It allows for flights, £150 per night accommodation and £25 a day for subsistence for both conferences. Other planned attendance at conferences, JIVDERCA and SMA Europe, will be supported by other funding sources, for example the learning academy at Great Ormond Street Hospital. Within travel, this also accounts for 2 international visits to Bergen and Milan for training. It allows for flights, £150 per night accommodation and £25 a day for subsistence. It also allows for a visit to the National hospital for Neurology and neurosurgery. Equipment The equipment costs are in year one. The cost is for rental of equipment associated with transnasal flexible laryngoscopes. This is required equipment to complete the transnasal fibreoptic laryngoscopes described in work package 1. Leasing cost involves part purchase and part loan totalling £34375.20 for a 6-month period VS. a purchase price which would otherwise be £54350.40 Other equipment consists of an encrypted hard drive and memory stick, which is required for storag

e and back up of all project data. Cost is based on 2TB (£390) external hard drive and a 128GB memory stick (£70). A laptop (£1000) has also been costed in the equipment section. Consumables The costs include: - Cleaning of the Transnasal fibreoptic Laryngoscopes ( £38 per scope for 50 patients = £1900) - Double sided stickers for opto-electronic plethysmography: Single use stickers for the OEP, 52 stickers required per patients per visit = 2,600 stickers required (£60 per 500) - Printing costs for posters for conference - Printing costs for study related materials Knowledge mobilisation, dissemination and impact Costings include production of podcasts and talking heads production, to ensure equitable dissemination of information. Training and development Courses included in the career development plan are a combination of free and paid for courses. PPIE workshops and the statistics course are free to GOSH/UCL staff. This section accounts for the PhD tuition fees of £23048.40 for 3 years. Paid for courses include for a total cost of £4682.40; - The Kings Fund leadership course: The programme will equip leaders with the skills needed to manage a team, build resilience and respond to

f charge - Public Involvement in Research | Coursera. Include are also costings for refreshments for the study participants and families. Other direct costs The cost associated with this section is related to software required for upload of the videos from the new assessments. It is through ChilliPharm, a secure online platform. The cost includes 1 instance of access to ChilliPharm , 1 iPad filming kit to be used for the external patient videos provided for free, support with synchronisation of videos from the room and the TFL to allow for data analysis (per video synchronisation £520) with set up costs of £3600. evidence.text:
Research Question
Can imaging of airway dynamics optimise assisted coughing in children with spinal muscular atrophy?
Background
Spinal Muscular Atrophy(SMA) is a life-limiting condition affecting children’s ability to move limbs,
swallow, breathe and cough effectively, impacting quality of life and life expectancy. Patients develop
increased secretions, causing recurrent chest infections and hospital admissions. Respiratory
physiotherapy relies on Mechanical Insufflation Exsufflation (MI-E) devices to help prevent infections.
MI-E delivers positive pressure promoting deep inhalation followed by a rapid switch to negative
pressure to mimic a cough. MI-E are programmed on specific settings for children to use at home.
However, there is limited understanding of what occurs in the child’s airway when breathing, coughing,
and using MI-E. Concerns include potential airway collapse and MI-E effectiveness. Understanding
upper airway response to using MI-E is vital to improve patient outcomes
Aims
Investigate reasons for variability in effectiveness of MI-E in children, establishing optimal settings for
MI-E in patients with SMA
Assess variations in lung function with optimised MI-E settin

gs
Evaluate tolerance and acceptance of new assessment techniques
Objectives
Work Package(WP)1a: Assess the upper airway dynamics using Transnasal Fibreoptic
Laryngoscope(TFL) in children with SMA
WP1b: Evaluate/optimise upper airway response patterns to MI-E
WP2: Assess changes in respiratory patterns/lung volumes utilising opto-electronic
plethysmography(OEP) with baseline and optimised MI-E settings
WP3: Measure patients'/carers' tolerance and acceptability of assessment techniques
Method
An interventional quantitative study consisting of three WPs
45 patients will be recruited. In WP1 Patients will be assessed with TFL during normal breathing and use
of home MI-E settings. Airway status will then be analysed with MI-E being adjusted for optimal airway
status. In WP2, OEP will measure differences in breathing patterns and lung volumes, before MI-E, at

gs
Evaluate tolerance and acceptance of new assessment techniques
Objectives
Work Package(WP)1a: Assess the upper airway dynamics using Transnasal Fibreoptic
Laryngoscope(TFL) in children with SMA
WP1b: Evaluate/optimise upper airway response patterns to MI-E
WP2: Assess changes in respiratory patterns/lung volumes utilising opto-electronic
plethysmography(OEP) with baseline and optimised MI-E settings
WP3: Measure patients'/carers' tolerance and acceptability of assessment techniques
Method
An interventional quantitative study consisting of three WPs
45 patients will be recruited. In WP1 Patients will be assessed with TFL during normal breathing and use
of home MI-E settings. Airway status will then be analysed with MI-E being adjusted for optimal airway
status. In WP2, OEP will measure differences in breathing patterns and lung volumes, before MI-E, at
baseline MI-E settings and on adjusted MI-E settings. During WP1 and WP2, patients/carers will rate
tests in terms of tolerance and acceptance on a visual scale (WP3). Statistical analysis will be
conducted. Data synthesis will confirm challenges, strengths, and areas for improvement.
Timelines for delivery
WP1, 2 and 3: 9-18Months

evidence.text:
Knowledge mobilisation, dissemination, and impact
Improved secretion management will reduce hospitalisations improving health and wellbeing. The study
provides insight into airway dynamics and will translate into other paediatric conditions. Results will be
shared via podcasts and meetings with parent support groups/charities, and presentations at
meetings/conferences, throughout the UK and internationally, including submission to appropriate peer
reviewed journals.
Research Inclusion
The study focuses on paediatric patients with SMA, which as a rare disease serves an under-
represented population from all cultural backgrounds. It is supported by community steering groups.
Adjustments have been made to ensure patients with behavioural and communicative concerns are able
to participate. Patient information sheets will be available to all and adapted for different communication
styles. An animated video has been created to aid inclusion. Characteristics related to inclusion of
participants will be reported. Ongoing reviews will ensure equality, diversity and inclusion are
considered.

Spinal Muscular Atrophy (SMA) is a condition which affects how long you can live, mostly affecting
children. SMA weakens muscles, including those needed to move limbs, swallow, breathe and cough.
As a result of weakened breathing muscles, a thick and sticky substance known as mucus builds up in
the child’s lungs, causing difficulty breathing. This leads to infections in their lungs and repeated hospital
visits affecting children’s quality of life and life expectancy.
Physiotherapists work with these children and their parents to prevent them from becoming unwell. One
way these children can be helped is by daily use of a machine referred to as a cough-assist. This mimics
a cough clearing mucus from the lungs. A child needs to wear a mask for a short time over their nose
and mouth as air is blown into their lungs. This helps them take a big breath in, followed by creating a
vacuum to move mucus out.
In SMA, we need to understand what happens to the child’s airway (breathing tube) whilst breathing,
during coughing and when using cough-assist.
We need more knowledge about how cough-assist settings help improve mucus clearance, reducing the
risk of chest infections, improving quality of

evidence.text:
Research Question
Can imaging of airway dynamics optimise assisted coughing in children with spinal muscular atrophy?
Background
Spinal Muscular Atrophy(SMA) is a life-limiting condition affecting children’s ability to move limbs,
swallow, breathe and cough effectively, impacting quality of life and life expectancy. Patients develop
increased secretions, causing recurrent chest infections and hospital admissions. Respiratory
physiotherapy relies on Mechanical Insufflation Exsufflation (MI-E) devices to help prevent infections.
MI-E delivers positive pressure promoting deep inhalation followed by a rapid switch to negative
pressure to mimic a cough. MI-E are programmed on specific settings for children to use at home.
However, there is limited understanding of what occurs in the child’s airway when breathing, coughing,
and using MI-E. Concerns include potential airway collapse and MI-E effectiveness. Understanding
upper airway response to using MI-E is vital to improve patient outcomes
Aims
Investigate reasons for variability in effectiveness of MI-E in children, establishing optimal settings for
MI-E in patients with SMA
Assess variations in lung function with optimised MI-E settin

criteria.rationale: While the courses are well-described, the application lacks a structured timeline or sequencing of when these training activities will occur relative to the project phases. The description is a list of budgeted items rather than a chronological training plan.

criteria.definition: How well the proposed training addresses the applicant’s current skills gaps and supports successful project delivery.

Mention of mentorship, workshops, or formal courses relevant to development needs

criteria.pros: The training plan includes courses with high transferability, such as the Kings Fund leadership course and the Impact Acceleration Unit course on knowledge mobilisation. These directly support a long-term career trajectory in clinical academia by building management and dissemination skills beyond the immediate research project.

sub_criteria.name: Track Record of the Contracting Organisation (and Partners)
sub_criteria.definition: Demonstrated expertise, experience, and research achievements of the organisation(s) in the proposed research area.

signals.signal_text: Publication or project history in relevant research fields
Previous funding success or awards in similar domains
Mention of prior collaborations or partnerships
Highlighted achievements or notable outputs

sub_criteria.pros: The application provides strong evidence of the contracting organisation's track record. GOSH and UCL are described as having the 'largest concentration of children's health research outside North America' and hosting the 'largest paediatric neuromuscular centre in the UK'. Specific achievements include the Dubowitz Neuromuscular Centre, the ORCHID research centre, and active involvement in international consortia (International SMA Consortium). Supervisors highlight extensive prior funding (NIHR, Western Norway Health Authorities) and successful completions of PhDs and MScs.
sub_criteria.drawbacks: While the track record is extensive, specific metrics regarding the number of publications or exact funding amounts secured by the organisation in the last 5 years are not explicitly quantified in the text, though the qualitative description is very strong.
sub_criteria.rationale: While the track record is extensive, specific metrics regarding the number of publications or exact funding amounts secured by the organisation in the last 5 years are not explicitly quantified in the text, though the qualitative description is very strong.

evidence.text:
SUMMARY INFORMATION > Application Title:
Can imaging of airway dynamics optimise assisted coughing in children with spinal muscular atrophy?

SUMMARY INFORMATION > Contracting Organisation:
GREAT ORMOND STREET HOSPITAL FOR CHILDREN NHS FOUNDATION TRUST

SUMMARY INFORMATION > Start Date:
01/04/2026

SUMMARY INFORMATION > End Date:
31/03/2029

SUMMARY INFORMATION > Duration (months):
36

SUMMARY INFORMATION > Has this application been previously submitted to this or any other funding body?:
Yes

LEAD APPLICANT & RESEARCH TEAM > Lead Applicant > Full Name:
Mrs Lisa Edel

LEAD APPLICANT & RESEARCH TEAM > Lead Applicant > Organisation:
GREAT ORMOND STREET HOSPITAL FOR CHILDREN NHS FOUNDATION TRUST

LEAD APPLICANT & RESEARCH TEAM > Lead Applicant > Department:
Physiotherapy Professional Background Allied Health Professional Detailed Professional Physiotherapist Background Professional Registration HCPC: PH88636

LEAD APPLICANT & RESEARCH TEAM > Lead Applicant > ORCID:
0000-0002-9352-5388

SUPPORT AND MENTORSHIP:
Organisation Western Norway University of Applied Sciences Email Address tiina.maarit.andersen@helse-bergen.no Role Practice Supervisor Description of Support I have know Lisa since 2021 through her impressive contributions to the Provided field of paediatric respiratory physiotherapy, particularly her work with children affected by neuromuscular conditions. Her innovative approach to clinical practice and research in this niche area has gained international recognition and she has significantly contributed to improving patient care. Lisa has a robust research foundation prior to her PhD work, having participated in large-scale clinical trials and the development of novel respiratory outcome measures that are widely used internationally. Her dedication to translational research is evidenced by the implementation of her respiratory assessment tool in clinical settings worldwide, particularly for children with Spinal Muscular Atrophy. Her academic achievements, such as receiving the Lea Rose Award, further underscore her research capabilities. Additionally, her extensive international collaborations reflect her commitment to advancing standards of care and her ability to engage effectively with a global research community. Lisa has several supervisors, where most are located in the UK, where Lisa is collecting the data for the PhD. She has access to the patient population described in the project plan, and the equipment to perform the examinations. Additionally, I will contribute for Lisa to visit us and learn the method of transnasal fibreoptic laryngoscopy during MI-E manoeuvres from the research group that is currently world leader on this. I will support Lisa with the method of continuous laryngoscopy during the MI-E interventions, how to plan the examinations, perform them, and how to interpret the data, which is a method I developed in 2010. I developed the idea to use laryngoscopy to study the upper airways during dynamic physiotherapy treatment with a cough assist device. My PhD involved assessing the feasibility of the examination and determining normal findings in healthy individuals. Subsequently, I examined all Amyotrophic Lateral Sclerosis patients in our region and identified abnormalities in these complex cases. This was followed up to five years throughout disease progression, describing how these changes evolve over time. This research has significantly improved the clinical treatment of the most vulnerable and challenging patients with chronic respiratory failure. Following my PhD (2018), I continued working as a postdoctoral researcher with a 50% grant in 5 years from Western Norway Health Authorities, focusing on enhancing clinical respiratory support treatment for ALS patients. This multicentre study includes participants from Bergen, Norway, and Porto, Portugal. Additionally, we are investigating the role of the larynx in another motor neuron disease affecting both children and adults. Lisa project aligns with my ongoing areas of research. My research has particularly focused on the role of the upper airways during these techniques, with the goal of enhancing respiratory care for patients, and I consider myself to be very suitable support for Lisa’s project. I am currently one of the world's leading physiotherapists in the field of chronic respiratory failure, including serving as the physiotherapy representative in a small expert group that organizes the multidisciplinary World Congress on Home Mechanical Ventilation (JIVD Congress), as well as the "Respiratory Failure and Mechanical Ventilation" course under the auspices of the world's largest respiratory organization, the European Respiratory Society (ERS). Through many years of work in an international environment, I have also developed a significant interdisciplinary network across several countries, where we actively collaborate on everything from clinical reasoning to drafting international guidelines. After my PhD defence in 2018, I took on my first roles as a co- supervisor for two PhD students who successfully defended their theses in 2022 and 2024. Currently, I lead my own research group with four active PhD students, for whom I serve as the primary supervisor. This includes three physiotherapists and one medical doctor specializing in pulmonology. Additionally, I have supervised 10 master's students who have successfully completed their degrees and am currently supervising two additional master's students. Further, I have engaged as co-supervisor for Lisa Edel in her project in the planning phase. Full Name Dr Ema Swingwood Organisation UNIVERSITY HOSPITALS BRISTOL AND WESTON NHS FOUNDATION TRUST Email Address Ema.Swingwood@uwe.ac.uk Role Practice Supervisor Description of Support Alongside my clinical consultant role, I am a Clinical Research Fellow at Provided the University of the West of England (UWE) and a visiting lecturer at UWE and University College London for undergraduate and post- graduate Physiotherapy programmes. I have 20 years of clinical experience specifically in respiratory (both adults and paediatrics) and have been extensively involved in cough augmentation and airway clearance using the mechanical insufflation- exsufflation device, so have an excellent understanding of the clinical area that Lisa wishes to explore. I have lectured at International conferences over a number of years around these topics. Having completed my MSc in 2012, I have continued my research with my PhD work, which explored the use of MI-E in the intubated population. I currently hold an NIHR SCPRA award 2025-2027. I am the research lead for Diagnostics and Therapies at UHBW where a key component of my role is embedding research into the clinical setting and developing a research infrastructure for non-medics. My experiences with this will enable me to work effectively with Lisa during and beyond her PhD to develop a clinical academic role. I have supervised 9 MSc students (University College London) to completion, am supervising numerous ICA Internship applications, and am named as a clinical academic supervisor in another doctoral application. I have known Lisa for approximately five years, initially in a clinical capacity and through links within key national respiratory stakeholder groups, and more recently through informal mentoring. Lisa is a national expert in her field of the respiratory management of neuromuscular paediatrics patients. She has sought out opportunities to initially develop her research capability and research network. She is dedicated to optimising the care of her patients and her own development and I have no doubt that she will excel in this fellowship. I am thrilled to be able to support her and look forward to working with Lisa on this fascinating programme. Full Name Professor Giovanni Baranello Organisation University College London Email Address g.baranello@ucl.ac.uk Role Primary Academic Supervisor Description of Support I have had the opportunity to work closely with Lisa over the past 6 Provided years as neuromuscular consultant at the Great Ormond Street Hospital and we have collaborated on several projects, like the correlation between a clinical respiratory score and a more objective respiratory assessment using the optoelectronic plethysmography. Lisa has a strong interest in developing research in the respiratory management of children with neuromuscular diseases and has brilliant ideas on how to demonstrate that the respiratory physiotherapy management of airway clearance can lead to improved respiratory status of children with Spinal Muscular Atrophy (SMA). Lisa has shown herself to be intellectually excellent, hardworking, well- organized, and has a keen and enquiring mind. She has excellent written and oral presentation skills and interacts absolutely well with colleagues. She has authored a few publications, she is actively involved in national and international research networks, like the International SMA Consortium, that include research centres from the UK, Italy and the US, and has presented her research projects at national and international conferences. I am committed to provide Lisa with all the necessary supervision during her PhD project, should she be successful in her application. I have the time and expertise to do so. As clinician academic I regularly provide supervision to postgraduate students. In the last 5 years I have supervised 5 PhD students, 2 as primary, and 2 have achieved completion. The mission of the UCL Great Ormond Street Institute of Child Health where I am based is to improve the health and well-being of children, and the adults they will become, through world-class research, education and public engagement. The UCL GOS ICH, together with its clinical partner Great Ormond Street Hospital for Children, forms the largest concentration of children’s health research outside North America. The Dubowitz Neuromuscular Centre is the largest paediatric neuromuscular centre in the UK, and has a strong research interest in the causes and treatment of muscular dystrophies and other neuromuscular disorders in children. Along with myself, Professor Francesco Muntoni is also an academic member and lead of The Dubowitz Neuromuscular Centre. There are also other research members of the team that can provide support for Lisa’s research project, including 3 research physiotherapists and 3 clinical research fellows. I recommend Lisa Edel unreservedly for this PhD application. She is outstanding in terms of her personal and intellectual attributes. I am sure that she will be an excellent PhD student and will progress to a successful clinical-academic career. Full Name Dr Colin Butler Organisation GREAT ORMOND STREET HOSPITAL FOR CHILDREN NHS FOUNDATION TRUST Email Address colin.butler@gosh.nhs.uk Role Academic Supervisor Description of Support I am delighted to write in support Lisa Edel and her application. The Provided research proposal has the potential to significantly advance the care of children with Spinal Muscular Atrophy (SMA), where the focus of the research will be on understanding upper airway dynamicsechniques can be applied to improve mechanical insufflation and exsufflation in these children to improve and how novel t respiratory health. I am an academic ENT surgeon at Great Ormond Street Hospital (GOSH)and a senior clinical lecturer and PI of the EpiCENTR group at the Institute of Child Health at UCL. I have known Lisa for 2 years, both clinically and from a research perspective. At GOSH, she is a specialist in treating children with SMA, and her academic focus will undoubtedly make her a future leader in her field. The research proposal has the potential for high impact in improving respiratory health, not only in children with SMA but also has application to other pathologies. Lisa is in a unique position at Great Ormond Street to investigate children with this severe airway disease and has an excellent research track record to date. In relation to this proposal, I am pleased to offer assistance in supervising the clinical application of the endoscopy techniques proposed in the work and will support Lisa with the analysis and research methodologies outlined. Lisa will have access to the Ear Nose and Throat (ENT) research group at ICH, where there are weekly research group meetings with airway-based research clinicians.. The Institue of Child Health will provide Lisa appropriate postdoctoral research support, with appropriate training in place. My research group at ICH focuses on paediatric ENT research (clinical and laboratory based), where we have an active group of PhD and MSc students. I currently co-supervise 2 PhD students and 2 MSc students. I have completed supervision on 1 PhD student (completed within 4 years) and 2 MD students where they were awarded their degrees respectively. I also currently co-supervise a postdoctoral NIHR clinical lecturer in paediatric ENT. If awarded, Lisa will have regular supervision and be able gain peer support from the group of doctoral students linked to our group. A benefit of the cross-disciplinary approach will mean she will have support from several allied specialities. As an ENT consultant specialising in airway research, I can offer clinical training to Lisa in the endoscopy techniques required for the proposal and provide supportive analysis of the proposed in application. Our group has experience in both quantitative and As an ENT consultant specialising in airway research, I can provide clinical training to Lisa in the endoscopy techniques required for the proposal and offer supportive analysis of the proposed application. Our group has experience in both quantitative and qualitative research and in developing validated outcomes to assess airway pathologies. In short, I am enthusiastic about supporting Lisa and this project, which I believe has the potential to enhance healthcare, not only in the targeted area but also for children with significant respiratory diseases in general. Full Name Ms Deborah Ridout Organisation UCL Great Ormond Street Institute of Child Health Email Address d.ridout@ucl.ac.uk Role Academic Supervisor Description of Support As Director of the statistical support service at the Institute of Child Provided Health, I have more than 20 years of experience collaborating with colleagues throughout the Institute, UCL and with external academic and industry partners. This has allowed me the opportunity to supervise and oversee many PhD and MSc students. In the last 6 years I have supervised 2 PhD students to completion and have been part of the supervisory panel for 6 others. One of my main areas of research is paediatric neuromuscular disease and I have collaborated extensively with experts in the field and have co-authored over 30 related publications. I have expertise in respiratory outcome measures in the patient population in the project and have supported many colleagues and students in understanding this type of data from a statistical perspective. I have helped design and set up many successful studies and am experienced in supporting and guiding students to develop their research skills. I am a statistics expert and can guide Lisa in her training and support her in applying her newly developed skills to her project. I have known Lisa for more than 6 years and found her to be a dedicated researcher who is keen and ambitious to explore new areas of research. I am committed to providing Lisa with all the necessary supervision during her PhD project, should she be successful in her application and have no doubt she will be an excellent student. Full Name Professor Dr Faith Gibson Organisation GREAT ORMOND STREET HOSPITAL FOR CHILDREN NHS FOUNDATION TRUST Email Address faith.gibson@gosh.nhs.uk Role Academic Supervisor Description of Support Lisa and I have worked together in terms of clinical academic activities Provided across the trust over the last 5 years. I am aware of Lisa’s profile and activities in research. I feel she is ready for a fellowship like this one and she is ready to move along in her chosen career of academic clinical research. She is ready to lead more research and support others in their research development. There is a lot of support for Lisa. She joins a thriving academic community. ORCHID is a research centre; Lisa is part of this centre and therefore benefits through interactions with PhD prepared nurses and Allied Health Professionals: in the clinical academic faculty we currently support 16 DCAF students, 5 PCAFs, 10 interns, and 13 post-docs, 1ACAF. We have a regular programme of activities for students, bringing them together with the Research Faculty: researchers on time- limited studies, that includes social scientists and health service researchers. We receive some support from the GOSH BRC and have an established vibrant internship programme and have huge success with the various NIHR programmes. My role as Director of this Centre is to actively support nurses and allied health professionals on their academic pathway, whoever is funding their fellowship, as well as supporting and mentoring other researchers within the Centre. To date I have been part of16 supervisory teams, I was primary supervisor for 7 of these: all successful and timely completions. I currently have 3 PhD students. I have undertaken 28 PhD vivas. My research expertise is qualitative and uses creative approaches to work with young children. My main field of research is children and young people who have cancer. I am a children’s cancer nurse with over 30 years’ experience in this speciality. My specific role is to support Lisa with her study design and working to develop questions to be used with families in finding out the tolerance, acceptability and comfort when receiving the intervention. Where there are no validated scales available, we have worked with PPI and communities, that includes healthcare professionals to devise suitable questions that help us to understand patient and family experiences. Families with lived experience in particular can help us to find the right questions, the right way to ask and when to ask. We have published some of this work, see for example: Kate Oulton, Naomi Oldrieve, Julie Bayliss, Victoria Jones, Isobel Manning, Lisa Shipway & Faith Gibson (2017): Using participatory and creative research methods to develop and pilot an informative game for preparing children for blood tests, Arts & Health, DOI:10.1080/17533015.2017.1392329

sub_criteria.name: Suitability of Supervisory Support and Mentorship Arrangements
sub_criteria.definition: Quality, clarity, and appropriateness of supervision and mentoring for supporting the applicant’s research and career development.

signals.signal_text: Explicit description of supervisor roles and responsibilities
Evidence of mentorship plans, meetings, or training support
Alignment between supervisor expertise and project needs
Presence of structured supervision framework (frequency, format, accountability)

sub_criteria.pros: The application details a robust, multi-disciplinary supervisory team with clearly defined roles: Primary Academic Supervisor (Prof Baranello), Academic Supervisors (Dr Butler, Dr Ridout, Prof Gibson), and Practice Supervisors (Dr Andersen, Dr Swingwood). Each supervisor explicitly outlines their specific contribution (e.g., statistical support, endoscopy training, clinical research infrastructure). Evidence of mentorship plans is strong, with mentions of weekly research group meetings, specific training in endoscopy techniques, and access to a thriving academic community with regular activities for students.
sub_criteria.drawbacks: While the roles and support are well-described, the specific frequency of one-on-one supervision meetings (e.g., weekly vs. monthly) is not explicitly stated for all supervisors, though 'regular supervision' is mentioned.
sub_criteria.rationale: While the roles and support are well-described, the specific frequency of one-on-one supervision meetings (e.g., weekly vs. monthly) is not explicitly stated for all supervisors, though 'regular supervision' is mentioned.
