I have 20 years of clinical experience specifically in respiratory (both adults and paediatrics) and have been extensively involved in cough augmentation and airway clearance using the mechanical insufflation- exsufflation device, so have an excellent understanding of the clinical area that Lisa wishes to explore. I have lectured at International conferences over a number of years around these topics. Having completed my MSc in 2012, I have continued my research with my PhD work, which explored the use of MI-E in the intubated population. I currently hold an NIHR SCPRA award 2025-2027. I am the research lead for Diagnostics and Therapies at UHBW where a key component of my role is embedding research into the clinical setting and developing a research infrastructure for non-medics. My experiences with this will enable me to work effectively with Lisa during and beyond her PhD to develop a clinical academic role. I have supervised 9 MSc students (University College London) to completion, am supervising numerous ICA Internship applications, and am named as a clinical academic supervisor in another doctoral application. I have known Lisa for approximately five years, initially in a clinical capacity and through links within key national respiratory stakeholder groups, and more recently through informal mentoring. Lisa is a national expert in her field of the respiratory management of neuromuscular paediatrics patients. She has sought out opportunities to initially develop her research capability and research network. She is dedicated to optimising the care of her patients and her own development and I have no doubt that she will excel in this fellowship. I am thrilled to be able to support her and look forward to working with Lisa on this fascinating programme. Full Name Professor Giovanni Baranello Organisation University College London Email Address g.baranello@ucl.ac.uk Role Primary Academic Supervisor Description of Support I have had the opportunity to work closely with Lisa over the past 6 Provided years as neuromuscular consultant at the Great Ormond Street Hospital and we have collaborated on several projects, like the correlation between a clinical respiratory score and a more objective respiratory assessment using the optoelectronic plethysmography. Lisa has a strong interest in developing research in the respiratory management of children with neuromuscular diseases and has brilliant ideas on how to demonstrate that the respiratory physiotherapy management of airway clearance can lead to improved respiratory status of children with Spinal Muscular Atrophy (SMA). Lisa has shown herself to be intellectually excellent, hardworking, well- organized, and has a keen and enquiring mind. She has excellent written and oral presentation skills and interacts absolutely well with colleagues. She has authored a few publications, she is actively involved in national and international research networks, like the International SMA Consortium, that include research centres from the UK, Italy and the US, and has presented her research projects at national and international conferences. I am committed to provide Lisa with all the necessary supervision during her PhD project, should she be successful in her application. I have the time and expertise to do so. As clinician academic I regularly provide supervision to postgraduate students. In the last 5 years I have supervised 5 PhD students, 2 as primary, and 2 have achieved completion. The mission of the UCL Great Ormond Street Institute of Child Health where I am based is to improve the health and well-being of children, and the adults they will become, through world-class research, education and public engagement. The UCL GOS ICH, together with its clinical partner Great Ormond Street Hospital for Children, forms the largest concentration of children’s health research outside North America. The Dubowitz Neuromuscular Centre is the largest paediatric neuromuscular centre in the UK, and has a strong research interest in the causes and treatment of muscular dystrophies and other neuromuscular disorders in children. Along with myself, Professor Francesco Muntoni is also an academic member and lead of The Dubowitz Neuromuscular Centre. There are also other research members of the team that can provide support for Lisa’s research project, including 3 research physiotherapists and 3 clinical research fellows. I recommend Lisa Edel unreservedly for this PhD application. She is outstanding in terms of her personal and intellectual attributes. I am sure that she will be an excellent PhD student and will progress to a successful clinical-academic career. Full Name Dr Colin Butler Organisation GREAT ORMOND STREET HOSPITAL FOR CHILDREN NHS FOUNDATION TRUST Email Address colin.butler@gosh.nhs.uk Role Academic Supervisor Description of Support I am delighted to write in support Lisa Edel and her application. The Provided research proposal has the potential to significantly advance the care of children with Spinal Muscular Atrophy (SMA), where the focus of the research will be on understanding upper airway dynamicsechniques can be applied to improve mechanical insufflation and exsufflation in these children to improve and how novel t respiratory health. I am an academic ENT surgeon at Great Ormond Street Hospital (GOSH)and a senior clinical lecturer and PI of the EpiCENTR group at the Institute of Child Health at UCL. I have known Lisa for 2 years, both clinically and from a research perspective. At GOSH, she is a specialist in treating children with SMA, and her academic focus will undoubtedly make her a future leader in her field. The research proposal has the potential for high impact in improving respiratory health, not only in children with SMA but also has application to other pathologies. Lisa is in a unique position at Great Ormond Street to investigate children with this severe airway disease and has an excellent research track record to date. In relation to this proposal, I am pleased to offer assistance in supervising the clinical application of the endoscopy techniques proposed in the work and will support Lisa with the analysis and research methodologies outlined. Lisa will have access to the Ear Nose and Throat (ENT) research group at ICH, where there are weekly research group meetings with airway-based research clinicians.. The Institue of Child Health will provide Lisa appropriate postdoctoral research support, with appropriate training in place. My research group at ICH focuses on paediatric ENT research (clinical and laboratory based), where we have an active group of PhD and MSc students. I currently co-supervise 2 PhD students and 2 MSc students. I have completed supervision on 1 PhD student (completed within 4 years) and 2 MD students where they were awarded their degrees respectively. I also currently co-supervise a postdoctoral NIHR clinical lecturer in paediatric ENT. If awarded, Lisa will have regular supervision and be able gain peer support from the group of doctoral students linked to our group. A benefit of the cross-disciplinary approach will mean she will have support from several allied specialities. As an ENT consultant specialising in airway research, I can offer clinical training to Lisa in the endoscopy techniques required for the proposal and provide supportive analysis of the proposed in application. Our group has experience in both quantitative and As an ENT consultant specialising in airway research, I can provide clinical training to Lisa in the endoscopy techniques required for the proposal and offer supportive analysis of the proposed application. Our group has experience in both quantitative and qualitative research and in developing validated outcomes to assess airway pathologies. In short, I am enthusiastic about supporting Lisa and this project, which I believe has the potential to enhance healthcare, not only in the targeted area but also for children with significant respiratory diseases in general. Full Name Ms Deborah Ridout Organisation UCL Great Ormond Street Institute of Child Health Email Address d.ridout@ucl.ac.uk Role Academic Supervisor Description of Support As Director of the statistical support service at the Institute of Child Provided Health, I have more than 20 years of experience collaborating with colleagues throughout the Institute, UCL and with external academic and industry partners. This has allowed me the opportunity to supervise and oversee many PhD and MSc students. In the last 6 years I have supervised 2 PhD students to completion and have been part of the supervisory panel for 6 others. One of my main areas of research is paediatric neuromuscular disease and I have collaborated extensively with experts in the field and have co-authored over 30 related publications. I have expertise in respiratory outcome measures in the patient population in the project and have supported many colleagues and students in understanding this type of data from a statistical perspective. I have helped design and set up many successful studies and am experienced in supporting and guiding students to develop their research skills. I am a statistics expert and can guide Lisa in her training and support her in applying her newly developed skills to her project. I have known Lisa for more than 6 years and found her to be a dedicated researcher who is keen and ambitious to explore new areas of research. I am committed to providing Lisa with all the necessary supervision during her PhD project, should she be successful in her application and have no doubt she will be an excellent student. Full Name Professor Dr Faith Gibson Organisation GREAT ORMOND STREET HOSPITAL FOR CHILDREN NHS FOUNDATION TRUST Email Address faith.gibson@gosh.nhs.uk Role Academic Supervisor Description of Support Lisa and I have worked together in terms of clinical academic activities Provided across the trust over the last 5 years. I am aware of Lisa’s profile and activities in research. I feel she is ready for a fellowship like this one and she is ready to move along in her chosen career of academic clinical research. She is ready to lead more research and support others in their research development. There is a lot of support for Lisa. She joins a thriving academic community. ORCHID is a research centre; Lisa is part of this centre and therefore benefits through interactions with PhD prepared nurses and Allied Health Professionals: in the clinical academic faculty we currently support 16 DCAF students, 5 PCAFs, 10 interns, and 13 post-docs, 1ACAF. We have a regular programme of activities for students, bringing them together with the Research Faculty: researchers on time- limited studies, that includes social scientists and health service researchers. We receive some support from the GOSH BRC and have an established vibrant internship programme and have huge success with the various NIHR programmes. My role as Director of this Centre is to actively support nurses and allied health professionals on their academic pathway, whoever is funding their fellowship, as well as supporting and mentoring other researchers within the Centre. To date I have been part of16 supervisory teams, I was primary supervisor for 7 of these: all successful and timely completions. I currently have 3 PhD students. I have undertaken 28 PhD vivas. My research expertise is qualitative and uses creative approaches to work with young children. My main field of research is children and young people who have cancer. I am a children’s cancer nurse with over 30 years’ experience in this speciality. My specific role is to support Lisa with her study design and working to develop questions to be used with families in finding out the tolerance, acceptability and comfort when receiving the intervention. Where there are no validated scales available, we have worked with PPI and communities, that includes healthcare professionals to devise suitable questions that help us to understand patient and family experiences. Families with lived experience in particular can help us to find the right questions, the right way to ask and when to ask. We have published some of this work, see for example: Kate Oulton, Naomi Oldrieve, Julie Bayliss, Victoria Jones, Isobel Manning, Lisa Shipway & Faith Gibson (2017): Using participatory and creative research methods to develop and pilot an informative game for preparing children for blood tests, Arts & Health, DOI:10.1080/17533015.2017.1392329

sub_criteria.name: Appropriateness of the Contracting Organisation to Support the Award and Career Aspirations
sub_criteria.definition: How well the organisation (and any partners) can provide the infrastructure, resources, and environment necessary to deliver the project and support the applicant’s career growth.

signals.signal_text: Description of facilities, infrastructure, or resources provided
Links between organisational support and project success
Alignment of organisational focus with applicant’s career trajectory
Evidence of tailored support for award type (doctoral, postdoctoral)

sub_criteria.pros: The application confirms access to critical resources: the patient population at GOSH, specific equipment (transnasal fibreoptic laryngoscopy, optoelectronic plethysmography), and the ORCHID research centre infrastructure. The organisational focus aligns perfectly with the applicant's career trajectory, with supervisors explicitly supporting her development into a clinical-academic role. Tailored support for the doctoral award is evident through the provision of statistical support services, research fellowships, and specific training in endoscopy and data analysis.
sub_criteria.drawbacks: No significant drawbacks; the evidence for facilities, resources, and alignment is comprehensive and directly linked to project success.
sub_criteria.rationale: No significant drawbacks; the evidence for facilities, resources, and alignment is comprehensive and directly linked to project success.

Can imaging of airway dynamics optimise assisted coughing in children with spinal muscular atrophy?

GREAT ORMOND STREET HOSPITAL FOR CHILDREN NHS FOUNDATION TRUST

Mrs Lisa Edel

LEAD APPLICANT & RESEARCH TEAM > Lead Applicant > Organisation:
GREAT ORMOND STREET HOSPITAL FOR CHILDREN NHS FOUNDATION TRUST

LEAD APPLICANT & RESEARCH TEAM > Lead Applicant > Department:
Physiotherapy Professional Background Allied Health Professional Detailed Professional Physiotherapist Background Professional Registration HCPC: PH88636

0000-0002-9352-5388

SUPPORT AND MENTORSHIP:
Organisation Western Norway University of Applied Sciences Email Address tiina.maarit.andersen@helse-bergen.no Role Practice Supervisor Description of Support I have know Lisa since 2021 through her impressive contributions to the Provided field of paediatric respiratory physiotherapy, particularly her work with children affected by neuromuscular conditions. Her innovative approach to clinical practice and research in this niche area has gained international recognition and she has significantly contributed to improving patient care. Lisa has a robust research foundation prior to her PhD work, having participated in large-scale clinical trials and the development of novel respiratory outcome measures that are widely used internationally. Her dedication to translational research is evidenced by the implementation of her respiratory assessment tool in clinical settings worldwide, particularly for children with Spinal Muscular Atrophy. Her academic achievements, such as receiving the Lea Rose Award, further underscore her research capabilities. Additionally, her extensive international collaborations reflect her commitment to advancing standards of care and her ability to engage effectively with a global research community. Lisa has several supervisors, where most are located in the UK, where Lisa is collecting the data for the PhD. She has access to the patient population described in the project plan, and the equipment to perform the examinations. Additionally, I will contribute for Lisa to visit us and learn the method of transnasal fibreoptic laryngoscopy during MI-E manoeuvres from the research group that is currently world leader on this. I will support Lisa with the method of continuous laryngoscopy during the MI-E interventions, how to plan the examinations, perform them, and how to interpret the data, which is a method I developed in 2010. I developed the idea to use laryngoscopy to study the upper airways during dynamic physiotherapy treatment with a cough assist device. My PhD involved assessing the feasibility of the examination and determining normal findings in healthy individuals. Subsequently, I examined all Amyotrophic Lateral Sclerosis patients in our region and identified abnormalities in these complex cases. This was followed up to five years throughout disease progression, describing how these changes evolve over time. This research has significantly improved the clinical treatment of the most vulnerable and challenging patients with chronic respiratory failure. Following my PhD (2018), I continued working as a postdoctoral researcher with a 50% grant in 5 years from Western Norway Health Authorities, focusing on enhancing clinical respiratory support treatment for ALS patients. This multicentre study includes participants from Bergen, Norway, and Porto, Portugal. Additionally, we are investigating the role of the larynx in another motor neuron disease affecting both children and adults. Lisa project aligns with my ongoing areas of research. My research has particularly focused on the role of the upper airways during these techniques, with the goal of enhancing respiratory care for patients, and I consider myself to be very suitable support for Lisa’s project. I am currently one of the world's leading physiotherapists in the field of chronic respiratory failure, including serving as the physiotherapy representative in a small expert group that organizes the multidisciplinary World Congress on Home Mechanical Ventilation (JIVD Congress), as well as the "Respiratory Failure and Mechanical Ventilation" course under the auspices of the world's largest respiratory organization, the European Respiratory Society (ERS). Through many years of work in an international environment, I have also developed a significant interdisciplinary network across several countries, where we actively collaborate on everything from clinical reasoning to drafting international guidelines. After my PhD defence in 2018, I took on my first roles as a co- supervisor for two PhD students who successfully defended their theses in 2022 and 2024. Currently, I lead my own research group with four active PhD students, for whom I serve as the primary supervisor. This includes three physiotherapists and one medical doctor specializing in pulmonology. Additionally, I have supervised 10 master's students who have successfully completed their degrees and am currently supervising two additional master's students. Further, I have engaged as co-supervisor for Lisa Edel in her project in the planning phase. sub_criteria.name: Commitment to Inclusive and Supportive Research Culture
sub_criteria.definition: Evidence that the organisation fosters inclusivity, research integrity, and a supportive environment for all researchers.

signals.signal_text: Mentions of policies or practices promoting diversity, equity, and inclusion
Evidence of inclusive recruitment, mentoring, or support initiatives
References to adherence to research integrity principles
Statements on organisational culture, training, or initiatives supporting underrepresented groups

sub_criteria.pros: There is some evidence of an inclusive culture through the mention of the ORCHID centre supporting a diverse group of students (nurses, AHPs) and the involvement of patient and public partners from diverse socio-economic and ethnic backgrounds in the research design. The supervisors mention a 'thriving academic community' and support for underrepresented groups in the context of patient involvement.
sub_criteria.drawbacks: The application lacks explicit mention of specific organisational policies promoting diversity, equity, and inclusion (DEI) within the research environment itself (e.g., staff training, specific DEI initiatives). While patient diversity is noted, the internal culture's commitment to inclusivity for researchers is implied rather than explicitly detailed with policy references or specific initiatives.
sub_criteria.rationale: The application lacks explicit mention of specific organisational policies promoting diversity, equity, and inclusion (DEI) within the research environment itself (e.g., staff training, specific DEI initiatives). While patient diversity is noted, the internal culture's commitment to inclusivity for researchers is implied rather than explicitly detailed with policy references or specific initiatives.

I am currently one of the world's leading physiotherapists in the field of chronic respiratory failure, including serving as the physiotherapy representative in a small expert group that organizes the multidisciplinary World Congress on Home Mechanical Ventilation (JIVD Congress), as well as the "Respiratory Failure and Mechanical Ventilation" course under the auspices of the world's largest respiratory organization, the European Respiratory Society (ERS). Through many years of work in an international environment, I have also developed a significant interdisciplinary network across several countries, where we actively collaborate on everything from clinical reasoning to drafting international guidelines. After my PhD defence in 2018, I took on my first roles as a co- supervisor for two PhD students who successfully defended their theses in 2022 and 2024. Currently, I lead my own research group with four active PhD students, for whom I serve as the primary supervisor. This includes three physiotherapists and one medical doctor specializing in pulmonology. Additionally, I have supervised 10 master's students who have successfully completed their degrees and am currently supervising two additional master's students. Further, I have engaged as co-supervisor for Lisa Edel in her project in the planning phase. Full Name Dr Ema Swingwood Organisation UNIVERSITY HOSPITALS BRISTOL AND WESTON NHS FOUNDATION TRUST Email Address Ema.Swingwood@uwe.ac.uk Role Practice Supervisor Description of Support Alongside my clinical consultant role, I am a Clinical Research Fellow at Provided the University of the West of England (UWE) and a visiting lecturer at UWE and University College London for undergraduate and post- graduate Physiotherapy programmes. I have 20 years of clinical experience specifically in respiratory (both adults and paediatrics) and have been extensively involved in cough augmentation and airway clearance using the mechanical insufflation- exsufflation device, so have an excellent understanding of the clinical area that Lisa wishes to explore. I have lectured at International conferences over a number of years around these topics. Having completed my MSc in 2012, I have continued my research with my PhD work, which explored the use of MI-E in the intubated population. I currently hold an NIHR SCPRA award 2025-2027. I am the research lead for Diagnostics and Therapies at UHBW where a key component of my role is embedding research into the clinical setting and developing a research infrastructure for non-medics. My experiences with this will enable me to work effectively with Lisa during and beyond her PhD to develop a clinical academic role. I have supervised 9 MSc students (University College London) to completion, am supervising numerous ICA Internship applications, and am named as a clinical academic supervisor in another doctoral application. I have known Lisa for approximately five years, initially in a clinical capacity and through links within key national respiratory stakeholder groups, and more recently through informal mentoring. Lisa is a national expert in her field of the respiratory management of neuromuscular paediatrics patients. She has sought out opportunities to initially develop her research capability and research network. She is dedicated to optimising the care of her patients and her own development and I have no doubt that she will excel in this fellowship. I am thrilled to be able to support her and look forward to working with Lisa on this fascinating programme. Full Name Professor Giovanni Baranello Organisation University College London Email Address g.baranello@ucl.ac.uk Role Primary Academic Supervisor Description of Support I have had the opportunity to work closely with Lisa over the past 6 Provided years as neuromuscular consultant at the Great Ormond Street Hospital and we have collaborated on several projects, like the correlation between a clinical respiratory score and a more objective respiratory assessment using the optoelectronic plethysmography. Lisa has a strong interest in developing research in the respiratory management of children with neuromuscular diseases and has brilliant ideas on how to demonstrate that the respiratory physiotherapy management of airway clearance can lead to improved respiratory status of children with Spinal Muscular Atrophy (SMA). Lisa has shown herself to be intellectually excellent, hardworking, well- organized, and has a keen and enquiring mind. She has excellent written and oral presentation skills and interacts absolutely well with colleagues. She has authored a few publications, she is actively involved in national and international research networks, like the International SMA Consortium, that include research centres from the UK, Italy and the US, and has presented her research projects at national and international conferences. I am committed to provide Lisa with all the necessary supervision during her PhD project, should she be successful in her application. I have the time and expertise to do so. As clinician academic I regularly provide supervision to postgraduate students. In the last 5 years I have supervised 5 PhD students, 2 as primary, and 2 have achieved completion. The mission of the UCL Great Ormond Street Institute of Child Health where I am based is to improve the health and well-being of children, and the adults they will become, through world-class research, education and public engagement. The UCL GOS ICH, together with its clinical partner Great Ormond Street Hospital for Children, forms the largest concentration of children’s health research outside North America. The Dubowitz Neuromuscular Centre is the largest paediatric neuromuscular centre in the UK, and has a strong research interest in the causes and treatment of muscular dystrophies and other neuromuscular disorders in children. Along with myself, Professor Francesco Muntoni is also an academic member and lead of The Dubowitz Neuromuscular Centre. There are also other research members of the team that can provide support for Lisa’s research project, including 3 research physiotherapists and 3 clinical research fellows. I recommend Lisa Edel unreservedly for this PhD application. She is outstanding in terms of her personal and intellectual attributes. I am sure that she will be an excellent PhD student and will progress to a successful clinical-academic career. Full Name Dr Colin Butler Organisation GREAT ORMOND STREET HOSPITAL FOR CHILDREN NHS FOUNDATION TRUST Email Address colin.butler@gosh.nhs.uk Role Academic Supervisor Description of Support I am delighted to write in support Lisa Edel and her application. The Provided research proposal has the potential to significantly advance the care of children with Spinal Muscular Atrophy (SMA), where the focus of the research will be on understanding upper airway dynamicsechniques can be applied to improve mechanical insufflation and exsufflation in these children to improve and how novel t respiratory health. I am an academic ENT surgeon at Great Ormond Street Hospital (GOSH)and a senior clinical lecturer and PI of the EpiCENTR group at the Institute of Child Health at UCL. I have known Lisa for 2 years, both clinically and from a research perspective. At GOSH, she is a specialist in treating children with SMA, and her academic focus will undoubtedly make her a future leader in her field. The research proposal has the potential for high impact in improving respiratory health, not only in children with SMA but also has application to other pathologies. Lisa is in a unique position at Great Ormond Street to investigate children with this severe airway disease and has an excellent research track record to date. In relation to this proposal, I am pleased to offer assistance in supervising the clinical application of the endoscopy techniques proposed in the work and will support Lisa with the analysis and research methodologies outlined. Lisa will have access to the Ear Nose and Throat (ENT) research group at ICH, where there are weekly research group meetings with airway-based research clinicians.. The Institue of Child Health will provide Lisa appropriate postdoctoral research support, with appropriate training in place. My research group at ICH focuses on paediatric ENT research (clinical and laboratory based), where we have an active group of PhD and MSc students. I currently co-supervise 2 PhD students and 2 MSc students. I have completed supervision on 1 PhD student (completed within 4 years) and 2 MD students where they were awarded their degrees respectively. I also currently co-supervise a postdoctoral NIHR clinical lecturer in paediatric ENT. If awarded, Lisa will have regular supervision and be able gain peer support from the group of doctoral students linked to our group. A benefit of the cross-disciplinary approach will mean she will have support from several allied specialities. As an ENT consultant specialising in airway research, I can offer clinical training to Lisa in the endoscopy techniques required for the proposal and provide supportive analysis of the proposed in application. Our group has experience in both quantitative and As an ENT consultant specialising in airway research, I can provide clinical training to Lisa in the endoscopy techniques required for the proposal and offer supportive analysis of the proposed application. Our group has experience in both quantitative and qualitative research and in developing validated outcomes to assess airway pathologies. In short, I am enthusiastic about supporting Lisa and this project, which I believe has the potential to enhance healthcare, not only in the targeted area but also for children with significant respiratory diseases in general. Full Name Ms Deborah Ridout Organisation UCL Great Ormond Street Institute of Child Health Email Address d.ridout@ucl.ac.uk Role Academic Supervisor Description of Support As Director of the statistical support service at the Institute of Child Provided Health, I have more than 20 years of experience collaborating with colleagues throughout the Institute, UCL and with external academic and industry partners. This has allowed me the opportunity to supervise and oversee many PhD and MSc students. In the last 6 years I have supervised 2 PhD students to completion and have been part of the supervisory panel for 6 others. One of my main areas of research is paediatric neuromuscular disease and I have collaborated extensively with experts in the field and have co-authored over 30 related publications. I have expertise in respiratory outcome measures in the patient population in the project and have supported many colleagues and students in understanding this type of data from a statistical perspective. I have helped design and set up many successful studies and am experienced in supporting and guiding students to develop their research skills. I am a statistics expert and can guide Lisa in her training and support her in applying her newly developed skills to her project. I have known Lisa for more than 6 years and found her to be a dedicated researcher who is keen and ambitious to explore new areas of research. I am committed to providing Lisa with all the necessary supervision during her PhD project, should she be successful in her application and have no doubt she will be an excellent student. Full Name Professor Dr Faith Gibson Organisation GREAT ORMOND STREET HOSPITAL FOR CHILDREN NHS FOUNDATION TRUST Email Address faith.gibson@gosh.nhs.uk Role Academic Supervisor Description of Support Lisa and I have worked together in terms of clinical academic activities Provided across the trust over the last 5 years. I am aware of Lisa’s profile and activities in research. I feel she is ready for a fellowship like this one and she is ready to move along in her chosen career of academic clinical research. She is ready to lead more research and support others in their research development. There is a lot of support for Lisa. She joins a thriving academic community. ORCHID is a research centre; Lisa is part of this centre and therefore benefits through interactions with PhD prepared nurses and Allied Health Professionals: in the clinical academic faculty we currently support 16 DCAF students, 5 PCAFs, 10 interns, and 13 post-docs, 1ACAF. We have a regular programme of activities for students, bringing them together with the Research Faculty: researchers on time- limited studies, that includes social scientists and health service researchers. We receive some support from the GOSH BRC and have an established vibrant internship programme and have huge success with the various NIHR programmes. My role as Director of this Centre is to actively support nurses and allied health professionals on their academic pathway, whoever is funding their fellowship, as well as supporting and mentoring other researchers within the Centre. To date I have been part of16 supervisory teams, I was primary supervisor for 7 of these: all successful and timely completions. I currently have 3 PhD students. I have undertaken 28 PhD vivas. My research expertise is qualitative and uses creative approaches to work with young children. My main field of research is children and young people who have cancer. I am a children’s cancer nurse with over 30 years’ experience in this speciality. My specific role is to support Lisa with her study design and working to develop questions to be used with families in finding out the tolerance, acceptability and comfort when receiving the intervention. Where there are no validated scales available, we have worked with PPI and communities, that includes healthcare professionals to devise suitable questions that help us to understand patient and family experiences. Families with lived experience in particular can help us to find the right questions, the right way to ask and when to ask. We have published some of this work, see for example: Kate Oulton, Naomi Oldrieve, Julie Bayliss, Victoria Jones, Isobel Manning, Lisa Shipway & Faith Gibson (2017): Using participatory and creative research methods to develop and pilot an informative game for preparing children for blood tests, Arts & Health, DOI:10.1080/17533015.2017.1392329

criteria.name: Track Record of the Contracting Organisation (and Partners)
criteria.definition: Demonstrated expertise, experience, and research achievements of the organisation(s) in the proposed research area.

signals.signal_text: Publication or project history in relevant research fields
Previous funding success or awards in similar domains
Mention of prior collaborations or partnerships
Highlighted achievements or notable outputs

criteria.pros: The application provides strong evidence of the contracting organisation's track record. GOSH and UCL are described as having the 'largest concentration of children's health research outside North America' and hosting the 'largest paediatric neuromuscular centre in the UK'. Specific achievements include the Dubowitz Neuromuscular Centre, the ORCHID research centre, and active involvement in international consortia (International SMA Consortium). Supervisors highlight extensive prior funding (NIHR, Western Norway Health Authorities) and successful completions of PhDs and MScs.
criteria.drawbacks: While the track record is extensive, specific metrics regarding the number of publications or exact funding amounts secured by the organisation in the last 5 years are not explicitly quantified in the text, though the qualitative description is very strong.
criteria.rationale: While the track record is extensive, specific metrics regarding the number of publications or exact funding amounts secured by the organisation in the last 5 years are not explicitly quantified in the text, though the qualitative description is very strong.

Her innovative approach to clinical practice and research in this niche area has gained international recognition and she has significantly contributed to improving patient care. Lisa has a robust research foundation prior to her PhD work, having participated in large-scale clinical trials and the development of novel respiratory outcome measures that are widely used internationally. Her dedication to translational research is evidenced by the implementation of her respiratory assessment tool in clinical settings worldwide, particularly for children with Spinal Muscular Atrophy. Her academic achievements, such as receiving the Lea Rose Award, further underscore her research capabilities. Additionally, her extensive international collaborations reflect her commitment to advancing standards of care and her ability to engage effectively with a global research community. Lisa has several supervisors, where most are located in the UK, where Lisa is collecting the data for the PhD. She has access to the patient population described in the project plan, and the equipment to perform the examinations. Additionally, I will contribute for Lisa to visit us and learn the method of transnasal fibreoptic laryngoscopy during MI-E manoeuvres from the research group that is currently world leader on this. I will support Lisa with the method of continuous laryngoscopy during the MI-E interventions, how to plan the examinations, perform them, and how to interpret the data, which is a method I developed in 2010. I developed the idea to use laryngoscopy to study the upper airways during dynamic physiotherapy treatment with a cough assist device. My PhD involved assessing the feasibility of the examination and determining normal findings in healthy individuals. Subsequently, I examined all Amyotrophic Lateral Sclerosis patients in our region and identified abnormalities in these complex cases. This was followed up to five years throughout disease progression, describing how these changes evolve over time. This research has significantly improved the clinical treatment of the most vulnerable and challenging patients with chronic respiratory failure. Following my PhD (2018), I continued working as a postdoctoral researcher with a 50% grant in 5 years from Western Norway Health Authorities, focusing on enhancing clinical respiratory support treatment for ALS patients. This multicentre study includes participants from Bergen, Norway, and Porto, Portugal. criteria.name: Suitability of Supervisory Support and Mentorship Arrangements
criteria.definition: Quality, clarity, and appropriateness of supervision and mentoring for supporting the applicant’s research and career development.

signals.signal_text: Explicit description of supervisor roles and responsibilities
Evidence of mentorship plans, meetings, or training support
Alignment between supervisor expertise and project needs
Presence of structured supervision framework (frequency, format, accountability)

criteria.pros: The application details a robust, multi-disciplinary supervisory team with clearly defined roles: Primary Academic Supervisor (Prof Baranello), Academic Supervisors (Dr Butler, Dr Ridout, Prof Gibson), and Practice Supervisors (Dr Andersen, Dr Swingwood). Each supervisor explicitly outlines their specific contribution (e.g., statistical support, endoscopy training, clinical research infrastructure). Evidence of mentorship plans is strong, with mentions of weekly research group meetings, specific training in endoscopy techniques, and access to a thriving academic community with regular activities for students.
criteria.drawbacks: While the roles and support are well-described, the specific frequency of one-on-one supervision meetings (e.g., weekly vs. monthly) is not explicitly stated for all supervisors, though 'regular supervision' is mentioned.
criteria.rationale: While the roles and support are well-described, the specific frequency of one-on-one supervision meetings (e.g., weekly vs. monthly) is not explicitly stated for all supervisors, though 'regular supervision' is mentioned.

I will support Lisa with the method of continuous laryngoscopy during the MI-E interventions, how to plan the examinations, perform them, and how to interpret the data, which is a method I developed in 2010. I developed the idea to use laryngoscopy to study the upper airways during dynamic physiotherapy treatment with a cough assist device. My PhD involved assessing the feasibility of the examination and determining normal findings in healthy individuals. Subsequently, I examined all Amyotrophic Lateral Sclerosis patients in our region and identified abnormalities in these complex cases. This was followed up to five years throughout disease progression, describing how these changes evolve over time. This research has significantly improved the clinical treatment of the most vulnerable and challenging patients with chronic respiratory failure. Following my PhD (2018), I continued working as a postdoctoral researcher with a 50% grant in 5 years from Western Norway Health Authorities, focusing on enhancing clinical respiratory support treatment for ALS patients. This multicentre study includes participants from Bergen, Norway, and Porto, Portugal. Additionally, we are investigating the role of the larynx in another motor neuron disease affecting both children and adults. Lisa project aligns with my ongoing areas of research. My research has particularly focused on the role of the upper airways during these techniques, with the goal of enhancing respiratory care for patients, and I consider myself to be very suitable support for Lisa’s project. I am currently one of the world's leading physiotherapists in the field of chronic respiratory failure, including serving as the physiotherapy representative in a small expert group that organizes the multidisciplinary World Congress on Home Mechanical Ventilation (JIVD Congress), as well as the "Respiratory Failure and Mechanical Ventilation" course under the auspices of the world's largest respiratory organization, the European Respiratory Society (ERS). Through many years of work in an international environment, I have also developed a significant interdisciplinary network across several countries, where we actively collaborate on everything from clinical reasoning to drafting international guidelines. After my PhD defence in 2018, I took on my first roles as a co- supervisor for two PhD students who successfully defended their theses in 2022 and 2024. Currently, I lead my own research group with four active PhD students, for whom I serve as the primary supervisor. This includes three physiotherapists and one medical doctor specializing in pulmonology. Additionally, I have supervised 10 master's students who have successfully completed their degrees and am currently supervising two additional master's students. Further, I have engaged as co-supervisor for Lisa Edel in her project in the planning phase. Full Name Dr Ema Swingwood Organisation UNIVERSITY HOSPITALS BRISTOL AND WESTON NHS FOUNDATION TRUST Email Address Ema.Swingwood@uwe.ac.uk Role Practice Supervisor Description of Support Alongside my clinical consultant role, I am a Clinical Research Fellow at Provided the University of the West of England (UWE) and a visiting lecturer at UWE and University College London for undergraduate and post- graduate Physiotherapy programmes. I have 20 years of clinical experience specifically in respiratory (both adults and paediatrics) and have been extensively involved in cough augmentation and airway clearance using the mechanical insufflation- exsufflation device, so have an excellent understanding of the clinical area that Lisa wishes to explore. I have lectured at International conferences over a number of years around these topics. Having completed my MSc in 2012, I have continued my research with my PhD work, which explored the use of MI-E in the intubated population. I currently hold an NIHR SCPRA award 2025-2027. I am the research lead for Diagnostics and Therapies at UHBW where a key component of my role is embedding research into the clinical setting and developing a research infrastructure for non-medics. My experiences with this will enable me to work effectively with Lisa during and beyond her PhD to develop a clinical academic role. I have supervised 9 MSc students (University College London) to completion, am supervising numerous ICA Internship applications, and am named as a clinical academic supervisor in another doctoral application. I have known Lisa for approximately five years, initially in a clinical capacity and through links within key national respiratory stakeholder groups, and more recently through informal mentoring. Lisa is a national expert in her field of the respiratory management of neuromuscular paediatrics patients. She has sought out opportunities to initially develop her research capability and research network. She is dedicated to optimising the care of her patients and her own development and I have no doubt that she will excel in this fellowship. I am thrilled to be able to support her and look forward to working with Lisa on this fascinating programme. Full Name Professor Giovanni Baranello Organisation University College London Email Address g.baranello@ucl.ac.uk Role Primary Academic Supervisor Description of Support I have had the opportunity to work closely with Lisa over the past 6 Provided years as neuromuscular consultant at the Great Ormond Street Hospital and we have collaborated on several projects, like the correlation between a clinical respiratory score and a more objective respiratory assessment using the optoelectronic plethysmography. Lisa has a strong interest in developing research in the respiratory management of children with neuromuscular diseases and has brilliant ideas on how to demonstrate that the respiratory physiotherapy management of airway clearance can lead to improved respiratory status of children with Spinal Muscular Atrophy (SMA). Lisa has shown herself to be intellectually excellent, hardworking, well- organized, and has a keen and enquiring mind. She has excellent written and oral presentation skills and interacts absolutely well with colleagues. She has authored a few publications, she is actively involved in national and international research networks, like the International SMA Consortium, that include research centres from the UK, Italy and the US, and has presented her research projects at national and international conferences. I am committed to provide Lisa with all the necessary supervision during her PhD project, should she be successful in her application. I have the time and expertise to do so. As clinician academic I regularly provide supervision to postgraduate students. In the last 5 years I have supervised 5 PhD students, 2 as primary, and 2 have achieved completion. The mission of the UCL Great Ormond Street Institute of Child Health where I am based is to improve the health and well-being of children, and the adults they will become, through world-class research, education and public engagement. The UCL GOS ICH, together with its clinical partner Great Ormond Street Hospital for Children, forms the largest concentration of children’s health research outside North America. The Dubowitz Neuromuscular Centre is the largest paediatric neuromuscular centre in the UK, and has a strong research interest in the causes and treatment of muscular dystrophies and other neuromuscular disorders in children. Along with myself, Professor Francesco Muntoni is also an academic member and lead of The Dubowitz Neuromuscular Centre. There are also other research members of the team that can provide support for Lisa’s research project, including 3 research physiotherapists and 3 clinical research fellows. I recommend Lisa Edel unreservedly for this PhD application. She is outstanding in terms of her personal and intellectual attributes. I am sure that she will be an excellent PhD student and will progress to a successful clinical-academic career. Full Name Dr Colin Butler Organisation GREAT ORMOND STREET HOSPITAL FOR CHILDREN NHS FOUNDATION TRUST Email Address colin.butler@gosh.nhs.uk Role Academic Supervisor Description of Support I am delighted to write in support Lisa Edel and her application. The Provided research proposal has the potential to significantly advance the care of children with Spinal Muscular Atrophy (SMA), where the focus of the research will be on understanding upper airway dynamicsechniques can be applied to improve mechanical insufflation and exsufflation in these children to improve and how novel t respiratory health. I am an academic ENT surgeon at Great Ormond Street Hospital (GOSH)and a senior clinical lecturer and PI of the EpiCENTR group at the Institute of Child Health at UCL. I have known Lisa for 2 years, both clinically and from a research perspective. At GOSH, she is a specialist in treating children with SMA, and her academic focus will undoubtedly make her a future leader in her field. The research proposal has the potential for high impact in improving respiratory health, not only in children with SMA but also has application to other pathologies. Lisa is in a unique position at Great Ormond Street to investigate children with this severe airway disease and has an excellent research track record to date. In relation to this proposal, I am pleased to offer assistance in supervising the clinical application of the endoscopy techniques proposed in the work and will support Lisa with the analysis and research methodologies outlined. Lisa will have access to the Ear Nose and Throat (ENT) research group at ICH, where there are weekly research group meetings with airway-based research clinicians.. The Institue of Child Health will provide Lisa appropriate postdoctoral research support, with appropriate training in place. My research group at ICH focuses on paediatric ENT research (clinical and laboratory based), where we have an active group of PhD and MSc students. I currently co-supervise 2 PhD students and 2 MSc students. I have completed supervision on 1 PhD student (completed within 4 years) and 2 MD students where they were awarded their degrees respectively. I also currently co-supervise a postdoctoral NIHR clinical lecturer in paediatric ENT. If awarded, Lisa will have regular supervision and be able gain peer support from the group of doctoral students linked to our group. A benefit of the cross-disciplinary approach will mean she will have support from several allied specialities. As an ENT consultant specialising in airway research, I can offer clinical training to Lisa in the endoscopy techniques required for the proposal and provide supportive analysis of the proposed in application. Our group has experience in both quantitative and As an ENT consultant specialising in airway research, I can provide clinical training to Lisa in the endoscopy techniques required for the proposal and offer supportive analysis of the proposed application. Our group has experience in both quantitative and qualitative research and in developing validated outcomes to assess airway pathologies. In short, I am enthusiastic about supporting Lisa and this project, which I believe has the potential to enhance healthcare, not only in the targeted area but also for children with significant respiratory diseases in general. Full Name Ms Deborah Ridout Organisation UCL Great Ormond Street Institute of Child Health Email Address d.ridout@ucl.ac.uk Role Academic Supervisor Description of Support As Director of the statistical support service at the Institute of Child Provided Health, I have more than 20 years of experience collaborating with colleagues throughout the Institute, UCL and with external academic and industry partners. This has allowed me the opportunity to supervise and oversee many PhD and MSc students. In the last 6 years I have supervised 2 PhD students to completion and have been part of the supervisory panel for 6 others. One of my main areas of research is paediatric neuromuscular disease and I have collaborated extensively with experts in the field and have co-authored over 30 related publications. I have expertise in respiratory outcome measures in the patient population in the project and have supported many colleagues and students in understanding this type of data from a statistical perspective. I have helped design and set up many successful studies and am experienced in supporting and guiding students to develop their research skills. I am a statistics expert and can guide Lisa in her training and support her in applying her newly developed skills to her project. I have known Lisa for more than 6 years and found her to be a dedicated researcher who is keen and ambitious to explore new areas of research. I am committed to providing Lisa with all the necessary supervision during her PhD project, should she be successful in her application and have no doubt she will be an excellent student. Full Name Professor Dr Faith Gibson Organisation GREAT ORMOND STREET HOSPITAL FOR CHILDREN NHS FOUNDATION TRUST Email Address faith.gibson@gosh.nhs.uk Role Academic Supervisor Description of Support Lisa and I have worked together in terms of clinical academic activities Provided across the trust over the last 5 years. I am aware of Lisa’s profile and activities in research. I feel she is ready for a fellowship like this one and she is ready to move along in her chosen career of academic clinical research. She is ready to lead more research and support others in their research development. There is a lot of support for Lisa. She joins a thriving academic community. ORCHID is a research centre; Lisa is part of this centre and therefore benefits through interactions with PhD prepared nurses and Allied Health Professionals: in the clinical academic faculty we currently support 16 DCAF students, 5 PCAFs, 10 interns, and 13 post-docs, 1ACAF. We have a regular programme of activities for students, bringing them together with the Research Faculty: researchers on time- limited studies, that includes social scientists and health service researchers. We receive some support from the GOSH BRC and have an established vibrant internship programme and have huge success with the various NIHR programmes. My role as Director of this Centre is to actively support nurses and allied health professionals on their academic pathway, whoever is funding their fellowship, as well as supporting and mentoring other researchers within the Centre. To date I have been part of16 supervisory teams, I was primary supervisor for 7 of these: all successful and timely completions. I currently have 3 PhD students. I have undertaken 28 PhD vivas. My research expertise is qualitative and uses creative approaches to work with young children. My main field of research is children and young people who have cancer. I am a children’s cancer nurse with over 30 years’ experience in this speciality. My specific role is to support Lisa with her study design and working to develop questions to be used with families in finding out the tolerance, acceptability and comfort when receiving the intervention. Where there are no validated scales available, we have worked with PPI and communities, that includes healthcare professionals to devise suitable questions that help us to understand patient and family experiences. Families with lived experience in particular can help us to find the right questions, the right way to ask and when to ask. We have published some of this work, see for example: Kate Oulton, Naomi Oldrieve, Julie Bayliss, Victoria Jones, Isobel Manning, Lisa Shipway & Faith Gibson (2017): Using participatory and creative research methods to develop and pilot an informative game for preparing children for blood tests, Arts & Health, DOI:10.1080/17533015.2017.1392329

criteria.name: Appropriateness of the Contracting Organisation to Support the Award and Career Aspirations
criteria.definition: How well the organisation (and any partners) can provide the infrastructure, resources, and environment necessary to deliver the project and support the applicant’s career growth.

signals.signal_text: Description of facilities, infrastructure, or resources provided
Links between organisational support and project success
Alignment of organisational focus with applicant’s career trajectory
Evidence of tailored support for award type (doctoral, postdoctoral)

criteria.pros: The application confirms access to critical resources: the patient population at GOSH, specific equipment (transnasal fibreoptic laryngoscopy, optoelectronic plethysmography), and the ORCHID research centre infrastructure. The organisational focus aligns perfectly with the applicant's career trajectory, with supervisors explicitly supporting her development into a clinical-academic role. Tailored support for the doctoral award is evident through the provision of statistical support services, research fellowships, and specific training in endoscopy and data analysis.
criteria.drawbacks: No significant drawbacks; the evidence for facilities, resources, and alignment is comprehensive and directly linked to project success.
criteria.rationale: No significant drawbacks; the evidence for facilities, resources, and alignment is comprehensive and directly linked to project success.

criteria.name: Commitment to Inclusive and Supportive Research Culture
criteria.definition: Evidence that the organisation fosters inclusivity, research integrity, and a supportive environment for all researchers.

signals.signal_text: Mentions of policies or practices promoting diversity, equity, and inclusion
Evidence of inclusive recruitment, mentoring, or support initiatives
References to adherence to research integrity principles
Statements on organisational culture, training, or initiatives supporting underrepresented groups

criteria.pros: There is some evidence of an inclusive culture through the mention of the ORCHID centre supporting a diverse group of students (nurses, AHPs) and the involvement of patient and public partners from diverse socio-economic and ethnic backgrounds in the research design. The supervisors mention a 'thriving academic community' and support for underrepresented groups in the context of patient involvement.
criteria.drawbacks: The application lacks explicit mention of specific organisational policies promoting diversity, equity, and inclusion (DEI) within the research environment itself (e.g., staff training, specific DEI initiatives). While patient diversity is noted, the internal culture's commitment to inclusivity for researchers is implied rather than explicitly detailed with policy references or specific initiatives.
criteria.rationale: The application lacks explicit mention of specific organisational policies promoting diversity, equity, and inclusion (DEI) within the research environment itself (e.g., staff training, specific DEI initiatives). While patient diversity is noted, the internal culture's commitment to inclusivity for researchers is implied rather than explicitly detailed with policy references or specific initiatives.
