SUPPORT AND MENTORSHIP:
Organisation Western Norway University of Applied Sciences Email Address tiina.maarit.andersen@helse-bergen.no Role Practice Supervisor Description of Support I have know Lisa since 2021 through her impressive contributions to the Provided field of paediatric respiratory physiotherapy, particularly her work with children affected by neuromuscular conditions. Her innovative approach to clinical practice and research in this niche area has gained international recognition and she has significantly contributed to improving patient care. Lisa has a robust research foundation prior to her PhD work, having participated in large-scale clinical trials and the development of novel respiratory outcome measures that are widely used internationally. Her dedication to translational research is evidenced by the implementation of her respiratory assessment tool in clinical settings worldwide, particularly for children with Spinal Muscular Atrophy. Her academic achievements, such as receiving the Lea Rose Award, further underscore her research capabilities. Additionally, her extensive international collaborations reflect her commitment to advancing standards of care and her ability to engage effectively with a global research community. Lisa has several supervisors, where most are located in the UK, where Lisa is collecting the data for the PhD. She has access to the patient population described in the project plan, and the equipment to perform the examinations. Additionally, I will contribute for Lisa to visit us and learn the method of transnasal fibreoptic laryngoscopy during MI-E manoeuvres from the research group that is currently world leader on this. I will support Lisa with the method of continuous laryngoscopy during the MI-E interventions, how to plan the examinations, perform them, and how to interpret the data, which is a method I developed in 2010. I developed the idea to use laryngoscopy to study the upper airways during dynamic physiotherapy treatment with a cough assist device. My PhD involved assessing the feasibility of the examination and determining normal findings in healthy individuals. Subsequently, I examined all Amyotrophic Lateral Sclerosis patients in our region and identified abnormalities in these complex cases. This was followed up to five years throughout disease progression, describing how these changes evolve over time. This research has significantly improved the clinical treatment of the most vulnerable and challenging patients with chronic respiratory failure. Following my PhD (2018), I continued working as a postdoctoral researcher with a 50% grant in 5 years from Western Norway Health Authorities, focusing on enhancing clinical respiratory support treatment for ALS patients. This multicentre study includes participants from Bergen, Norway, and Porto, Portugal. Additionally, we are investigating the role of the larynx in another motor neuron disease affecting both children and adults. Lisa project aligns with my ongoing areas of research. My research has particularly focused on the role of the upper airways during these techniques, with the goal of enhancing respiratory care for patients, and I consider myself to be very suitable support for Lisa’s project. I am currently one of the world's leading physiotherapists in the field of chronic respiratory failure, including serving as the physiotherapy representative in a small expert group that organizes the multidisciplinary World Congress on Home Mechanical Ventilation (JIVD Congress), as well as the "Respiratory Failure and Mechanical Ventilation" course under the auspices of the world's largest respiratory organization, the European Respiratory Society (ERS). Through many years of work in an international environment, I have also developed a significant interdisciplinary network across several countries, where we actively collaborate on everything from clinical reasoning to drafting international guidelines. After my PhD defence in 2018, I took on my first roles as a co- supervisor for two PhD students who successfully defended their theses in 2022 and 2024. Currently, I lead my own research group with four active PhD students, for whom I serve as the primary supervisor. This includes three physiotherapists and one medical doctor specializing in pulmonology. Additionally, I have supervised 10 master's students who have successfully completed their degrees and am currently supervising two additional master's students. Further, I have engaged as co-supervisor for Lisa Edel in her project in the planning phase. Full Name Dr Ema Swingwood Organisation UNIVERSITY HOSPITALS BRISTOL AND WESTON NHS FOUNDATION TRUST Email Address Ema.Swingwood@uwe.ac.uk Role Practice Supervisor Description of Support Alongside my clinical consultant role, I am a Clinical Research Fellow at Provided the University of the West of England (UWE) and a visiting lecturer at UWE and University College London for undergraduate and post- graduate Physiotherapy programmes. I have 20 years of clinical experience specifically in respiratory (both adults and paediatrics) and have been extensively involved in cough augmentation and airway clearance using the mechanical insufflation- exsufflation device, so have an excellent understanding of the clinical area that Lisa wishes to explore. I have lectured at International conferences over a number of years around these topics. Having completed my MSc in 2012, I have continued my research with my PhD work, which explored the use of MI-E in the intubated population. I currently hold an NIHR SCPRA award 2025-2027. I am the research lead for Diagnostics and Therapies at UHBW where a key component of my role is embedding research into the clinical setting and developing a research infrastructure for non-medics. My experiences with this will enable me to work effectively with Lisa during and beyond her PhD to develop a clinical academic role. I have supervised 9 MSc students (University College London) to completion, am supervising numerous ICA Internship applications, and am named as a clinical academic supervisor in another doctoral application. I have known Lisa for approximately five years, initially in a clinical capacity and through links within key national respiratory stakeholder groups, and more recently through informal mentoring. Lisa is a national expert in her field of the respiratory management of neuromuscular paediatrics patients. She has sought out opportunities to initially develop her research capability and research network. She is dedicated to optimising the care of her patients and her own development and I have no doubt that she will excel in this fellowship. I am thrilled to be able to support her and look forward to working with Lisa on this fascinating programme. Full Name Professor Giovanni Baranello Organisation University College London Email Address g.baranello@ucl.ac.uk Role Primary Academic Supervisor Description of Support I have had the opportunity to work closely with Lisa over the past 6 Provided years as neuromuscular consultant at the Great Ormond Street Hospital and we have collaborated on several projects, like the correlation between a clinical respiratory score and a more objective respiratory assessment using the optoelectronic plethysmography. Lisa has a strong interest in developing research in the respiratory management of children with neuromuscular diseases and has brilliant ideas on how to demonstrate that the respiratory physiotherapy management of airway clearance can lead to improved respiratory status of children with Spinal Muscular Atrophy (SMA). Lisa has shown herself to be intellectually excellent, hardworking, well- organized, and has a keen and enquiring mind. She has excellent written and oral presentation skills and interacts absolutely well with colleagues. She has authored a few publications, she is actively involved in national and international research networks, like the International SMA Consortium, that include research centres from the UK, Italy and the US, and has presented her research projects at national and international conferences. I am committed to provide Lisa with all the necessary supervision during her PhD project, should she be successful in her application. I have the time and expertise to do so. As clinician academic I regularly provide supervision to postgraduate students. In the last 5 years I have supervised 5 PhD students, 2 as primary, and 2 have achieved completion. The mission of the UCL Great Ormond Street Institute of Child Health where I am based is to improve the health and well-being of children, and the adults they will become, through world-class research, education and public engagement. The UCL GOS ICH, together with its clinical partner Great Ormond Street Hospital for Children, forms the largest concentration of children’s health research outside North America. The Dubowitz Neuromuscular Centre is the largest paediatric neuromuscular centre in the UK, and has a strong research interest in the causes and treatment of muscular dystrophies and other neuromuscular disorders in children. Along with myself, Professor Francesco Muntoni is also an academic member and lead of The Dubowitz Neuromuscular Centre. There are also other research members of the team that can provide support for Lisa’s research project, including 3 research physiotherapists and 3 clinical research fellows. I recommend Lisa Edel unreservedly for this PhD application. She is outstanding in terms of her personal and intellectual attributes. I am sure that she will be an excellent PhD student and will progress to a successful clinical-academic career. Full Name Dr Colin Butler Organisation GREAT ORMOND STREET HOSPITAL FOR CHILDREN NHS FOUNDATION TRUST Email Address colin.butler@gosh.nhs.uk Role Academic Supervisor Description of Support I am delighted to write in support Lisa Edel and her application. The Provided research proposal has the potential to significantly advance the care of children with Spinal Muscular Atrophy (SMA), where the focus of the research will be on understanding upper airway dynamicsechniques can be applied to improve mechanical insufflation and exsufflation in these children to improve and how novel t respiratory health. I am an academic ENT surgeon at Great Ormond Street Hospital (GOSH)and a senior clinical lecturer and PI of the EpiCENTR group at the Institute of Child Health at UCL. I have known Lisa for 2 years, both clinically and from a research perspective. At GOSH, she is a specialist in treating children with SMA, and her academic focus will undoubtedly make her a future leader in her field. The research proposal has the potential for high impact in improving respiratory health, not only in children with SMA but also has application to other pathologies. Lisa is in a unique position at Great Ormond Street to investigate children with this severe airway disease and has an excellent research track record to date. In relation to this proposal, I am pleased to offer assistance in supervising the clinical application of the endoscopy techniques proposed in the work and will support Lisa with the analysis and research methodologies outlined. Lisa will have access to the Ear Nose and Throat (ENT) research group at ICH, where there are weekly research group meetings with airway-based research clinicians.. The Institue of Child Health will provide Lisa appropriate postdoctoral research support, with appropriate training in place. My research group at ICH focuses on paediatric ENT research (clinical and laboratory based), where we have an active group of PhD and MSc students. I currently co-supervise 2 PhD students and 2 MSc students. I have completed supervision on 1 PhD student (completed within 4 years) and 2 MD students where they were awarded their degrees respectively. I also currently co-supervise a postdoctoral NIHR clinical lecturer in paediatric ENT. If awarded, Lisa will have regular supervision and be able gain peer support from the group of doctoral students linked to our group. A benefit of the cross-disciplinary approach will mean she will have support from several allied specialities. As an ENT consultant specialising in airway research, I can offer clinical training to Lisa in the endoscopy techniques required for the proposal and provide supportive analysis of the proposed in application. Our group has experience in both quantitative and As an ENT consultant specialising in airway research, I can provide clinical training to Lisa in the endoscopy techniques required for the proposal and offer supportive analysis of the proposed application. Our group has experience in both quantitative and qualitative research and in developing validated outcomes to assess airway pathologies. In short, I am enthusiastic about supporting Lisa and this project, which I believe has the potential to enhance healthcare, not only in the targeted area but also for children with significant respiratory diseases in general. Full Name Ms Deborah Ridout Organisation UCL Great Ormond Street Institute of Child Health Email Address d.ridout@ucl.ac.uk Role Academic Supervisor Description of Support As Director of the statistical support service at the Institute of Child Provided Health, I have more than 20 years of experience collaborating with colleagues throughout the Institute, UCL and with external academic and industry partners. This has allowed me the opportunity to supervise and oversee many PhD and MSc students. In the last 6 years I have supervised 2 PhD students to completion and have been part of the supervisory panel for 6 others. One of my main areas of research is paediatric neuromuscular disease and I have collaborated extensively with experts in the field and have co-authored over 30 related publications. I have expertise in respiratory outcome measures in the patient population in the project and have supported many colleagues and students in understanding this type of data from a statistical perspective. I have helped design and set up many successful studies and am experienced in supporting and guiding students to develop their research skills. I am a statistics expert and can guide Lisa in her training and support her in applying her newly developed skills to her project. I have known Lisa for more than 6 years and found her to be a dedicated researcher who is keen and ambitious to explore new areas of research. I am committed to providing Lisa with all the necessary supervision during her PhD project, should she be successful in her application and have no doubt she will be an excellent student. Full Name Professor Dr Faith Gibson Organisation GREAT ORMOND STREET HOSPITAL FOR CHILDREN NHS FOUNDATION TRUST Email Address faith.gibson@gosh.nhs.uk Role Academic Supervisor Description of Support Lisa and I have worked together in terms of clinical academic activities Provided across the trust over the last 5 years. I am aware of Lisa’s profile and activities in research. I feel she is ready for a fellowship like this one and she is ready to move along in her chosen career of academic clinical research. She is ready to lead more research and support others in their research development. There is a lot of support for Lisa. She joins a thriving academic community. ORCHID is a research centre; Lisa is part of this centre and therefore benefits through interactions with PhD prepared nurses and Allied Health Professionals: in the clinical academic faculty we currently support 16 DCAF students, 5 PCAFs, 10 interns, and 13 post-docs, 1ACAF. We have a regular programme of activities for students, bringing them together with the Research Faculty: researchers on time- limited studies, that includes social scientists and health service researchers. We receive some support from the GOSH BRC and have an established vibrant internship programme and have huge success with the various NIHR programmes. My role as Director of this Centre is to actively support nurses and allied health professionals on their academic pathway, whoever is funding their fellowship, as well as supporting and mentoring other researchers within the Centre. To date I have been part of16 supervisory teams, I was primary supervisor for 7 of these: all successful and timely completions. I currently have 3 PhD students. I have undertaken 28 PhD vivas. My research expertise is qualitative and uses creative approaches to work with young children. My main field of research is children and young people who have cancer. I am a children’s cancer nurse with over 30 years’ experience in this speciality. My specific role is to support Lisa with her study design and working to develop questions to be used with families in finding out the tolerance, acceptability and comfort when receiving the intervention. Where there are no validated scales available, we have worked with PPI and communities, that includes healthcare professionals to devise suitable questions that help us to understand patient and family experiences. Families with lived experience in particular can help us to find the right questions, the right way to ask and when to ask. We have published some of this work, see for example: Kate Oulton, Naomi Oldrieve, Julie Bayliss, Victoria Jones, Isobel Manning, Lisa Shipway & Faith Gibson (2017): Using participatory and creative research methods to develop and pilot an informative game for preparing children for blood tests, Arts & Health, DOI:10.1080/17533015.2017.1392329

sub_criteria.name: Integration of Lived Experience in Research Design
sub_criteria.definition: How input from patients, carers, or people with relevant lived experience has shaped the research questions, design, and methods.

signals.signal_text: Mentions of consultation or co-design activities
Specific examples of changes made to study design based on lived experience
References to patient or community advisory groups

sub_criteria.pros: The application provides robust evidence of co-design and consultation. Specific examples of design changes based on lived experience are detailed, including the creation of a child-friendly video for the TFL procedure, adjusting the testing schedule to manage fatigue, and extending data collection periods. A formal communities' group comprising patients, parents, and charity partners is established to guide the project.
sub_criteria.drawbacks: No material caveats or missing details identified; the evidence is comprehensive and directly addresses all signals.
sub_criteria.rationale: No material caveats or missing details identified; the evidence is comprehensive and directly addresses all signals.

evidence.text:
This proposal was developed in collaboration with SMA parents, carers and patients of Great Ormond
Street Hospital (GOSH) and charity partners. Medical colleagues from across the United Kingdom who
are linked to SMA were also consulted. Project proposal/relevance, study design and concerns were
shared.
A patient and communities’ group of 6 parents, 3 patients and 2 charity partners, aged 16-42 from
diverse socio-economic and ethnic backgrounds, were consulted on the plain English summary and
joined planning discussions. The Child and Young Person Group at GOSH contributed to the original
planning phase and plain English summary. The medical community stressed the important link between
the airway and lung volumes, and monitoring of patient tolerance to new tests. They highlighted this
work as visionary and a way to anticipate impact of the new phenotypes of SMA
The families confirmed importance of the research, highlighting it would help children stay out of
hospital, and their feedback has informed proposal development and design. Families reported it ‘gives
us a door to open up and see what is happening’ and ‘we are grateful you are looking into respiratory
care in SMA’.
Some par

ents raised concerns, and we revised/added activities to accommodate their feedback:
Transnasal fibreoptic laryngoscope (TFL): Concerns were regarding appearance, feel and what happens
if the child dislikes it. A child friendly video/cartoon explaining the procedure was suggested to help with
understanding. This has since been created. Patients requested to see the new equipment and all
equipment detailed in the study will be accessible to all patients.
Fatigue: The community requested testing for the TFL be completed in the morning, followed by the
opto-electronic plethysmography in the afternoon. We have ensured families will be able to take regular
breaks. The data collection period was made longer to allow for more spacing of patients to enable more
time per test.
Study Feedback: Regular feedback on the study was requested, ensuring it was appropriate and all-
inclusive. Coffee mornings/online meetings on study progression are planned.
Managing the Research
A communities’ group will be created, made up of 3 young adults living with SMA, 4 parents of children
with SMA and 3 representatives from charity partners. Recruitment will be through email
correspondence and social media,

sub_criteria.name: Diversity and Inclusivity of the Research Team
sub_criteria.definition: Extent to which the research team reflects diversity in terms of skills, experience, and demographic representation.

signals.signal_text: Explicit mentions of team composition across gender, ethnicity, age, or other relevant characteristics
Statements about inclusion in hiring or team-building
Evidence of equitable task allocation and collaboration

sub_criteria.pros: The PPI group is explicitly described as diverse, including 6 parents, 3 patients, and 2 charity partners aged 16-42 from diverse socio-economic and ethnic backgrounds. This directly supports the representation of diversity within the PPI team.
sub_criteria.drawbacks: While the PPI group is diverse, there is limited explicit evidence regarding the diversity of the core research team (supervisors/staff) beyond the PPI contributors. Statements about inclusion in hiring or equitable task allocation within the research team itself are not explicitly detailed.
sub_criteria.rationale: While the PPI group is diverse, there is limited explicit evidence regarding the diversity of the core research team (supervisors/staff) beyond the PPI contributors. Statements about inclusion in hiring or equitable task allocation within the research team itself are not explicitly detailed.

sub_criteria.name: Commitment to Maximising Public and Patient Benefit
sub_criteria.definition: Clarity and strength of the plan to ensure research outcomes meaningfully benefit patients, carers, and the public.

signals.signal_text: Mentions of dissemination, knowledge translation, or implementation plans
Clear links between study objectives and anticipated benefits
Emphasis on actionable outcomes or improvements in health/care

sub_criteria.pros: Dissemination plans are highly detailed and actionable, including podcasts, meetings with parent support groups, and charity networks. There are clear links between study objectives (optimising MI-E) and anticipated benefits (reduced hospitalisations, improved quality of life). The steering group is tasked with assisting in writing final reports and facilitating publication to ensure findings reach all audiences.
sub_criteria.drawbacks: No material caveats identified; the plan is strong and well-evidenced.
sub_criteria.rationale: No material caveats identified; the plan is strong and well-evidenced.

evidence.text:
Knowledge mobilisation, dissemination, and impact
Improved secretion management will reduce hospitalisations improving health and wellbeing. The study
provides insight into airway dynamics and will translate into other paediatric conditions. Results will be
shared via podcasts and meetings with parent support groups/charities, and presentations at
meetings/conferences, throughout the UK and internationally, including submission to appropriate peer
reviewed journals.
Research Inclusion
The study focuses on paediatric patients with SMA, which as a rare disease serves an under-
represented population from all cultural backgrounds. It is supported by community steering groups.
Adjustments have been made to ensure patients with behavioural and communicative concerns are able
to participate. Patient information sheets will be available to all and adapted for different communication
styles. An animated video has been created to aid inclusion. Characteristics related to inclusion of
participants will be reported. Ongoing reviews will ensure equality, diversity and inclusion are
considered.

ces in breathing patterns. This will highlight
any changes in the way children with SMA breath, having potential benefit in clearing more mucus out
from the lungs.
Part 3. Understand child's', parents' and carers' views, tolerance, and acceptance of the new
assessment techniques.
During the tests, children will be asked to rate the new tests in terms of acceptance, comfort, and
tolerance on a visual scale.
Sharing results
We will include children and their parents during the study. They will guide us in the best ways to share
information about what we find. We will also share what we find at meetings and conferences (In the
United Kingdom and internationally). We will write about what we learnt and share this through medical
and physiotherapy journals.

Parents/Patients/Charity Partners will be offered to:
• Participate in training for involvement in advisory groups, provided by Imperial College

Steering groups will assist with writing final reports/summaries of results and will be acknowledge for

dissemination through their informal networks will be invaluable to ensuring dissemination to all
audiences.

sub_criteria.name: Representation of Seldom Heard or Underserved Groups
sub_criteria.definition: Degree to which participants and PPI contributors represent groups historically underrepresented in research, including those facing health inequalities.

signals.signal_text: Mention of specific demographic groups (age, race, ethnicity, disability, gender, sexual orientation, religion/belief, location, socioeconomic status)
Evidence of targeted recruitment strategies
Discussion of barriers to participation and mitigation strategies

sub_criteria.pros: The application explicitly focuses on Spinal Muscular Atrophy (SMA), a rare disease affecting an under-represented population. The PPI group includes participants from diverse socio-economic and ethnic backgrounds. Adjustments for behavioural and communicative concerns are noted to ensure participation.
sub_criteria.drawbacks: While barriers like fatigue and communication are addressed, a more detailed discussion of specific recruitment strategies to overcome barriers for other underserved subgroups (e.g., specific geographic or socioeconomic barriers beyond the PPI group composition) could be more explicit.
sub_criteria.rationale: While barriers like fatigue and communication are addressed, a more detailed discussion of specific recruitment strategies to overcome barriers for other underserved subgroups (e.g., specific geographic or socioeconomic barriers beyond the PPI group composition) could be more explicit.

sub_criteria.name: Inclusivity Across the Research Lifecycle
sub_criteria.definition: Demonstration that inclusion is considered at all stages of the project: planning, recruitment, conduct, analysis, and dissemination.

signals.signal_text: References to inclusion in study design, participant selection, and analysis
Consideration of accessibility and equity in all project stages
Documented plans for ongoing evaluation and adaptation

sub_criteria.pros: Inclusion is demonstrated across the entire lifecycle: planning (co-design of PIS and methods), recruitment (adaptations for communication styles, animated videos), conduct (scheduling for fatigue, breaks), and dissemination (steering group involvement in reporting). Ongoing reviews for equality and diversity are planned.
sub_criteria.drawbacks: No material caveats identified; the evidence covers all stages of the research lifecycle comprehensively.
sub_criteria.rationale: No material caveats identified; the evidence covers all stages of the research lifecycle comprehensively.

sub_criteria.name: Costed and Resourced Plan for Research Inclusion
sub_criteria.definition: Evidence that research inclusion has been properly budgeted and resourced within the proposal.

signals.signal_text: Explicit budget line(s) for inclusion activities (PPI, outreach, recruitment support)
Mention of staff time, materials, or training dedicated to inclusion
Alignment between inclusion plan and allocated resources

sub_criteria.pros: The budget explicitly includes line items for inclusion activities: PPIE workshops, 'Talking Mats' for inclusive consent (£120 annual fee), and refreshments for study participants and families. Staff time and travel for committees are also costed. The resources align directly with the inclusion plan described in the PPI section.
sub_criteria.drawbacks: No material caveats identified; the budgeting is specific and well-aligned with the inclusion activities.
sub_criteria.rationale: No material caveats identified; the budgeting is specific and well-aligned with the inclusion activities.

evidence.text: an ever-changing external environment. - Bronchoscopy course at The Royal Brompton Hospital: A 2 day course for learning use of bronchoscopies (School of Bronchoscopy | Royal Brompton & Harefield hospitals) - Beyond BASIC Airway Management Course at The Royal Brompton Hospital: To increase skills with management of the upper airway (Beyond BASIC Airway Management Course | Royal Brompton & Harefield hospitals) - Impact Acceleration Unit, Knowledge mobilisation at Keele University: This is a fantastic opportunity for clinicians, academics and members of the public to improve their knowledge and understanding of the practical ways to design and use knowledge mobilisation theories and strategies in health and care practice. Research inclusion Courses related to research inclusion are included in the PhD programme and are free of charge. criteria.name: Integration of Lived Experience in Research Design
criteria.definition: How input from patients, carers, or people with relevant lived experience has shaped the research questions, design, and methods.

signals.signal_text: Mentions of consultation or co-design activities
Specific examples of changes made to study design based on lived experience
References to patient or community advisory groups

criteria.pros: The application provides robust evidence of co-design and consultation. Specific examples of design changes based on lived experience are detailed, including the creation of a child-friendly video for the TFL procedure, adjusting the testing schedule to manage fatigue, and extending data collection periods. A formal communities' group comprising patients, parents, and charity partners is established to guide the project.
criteria.drawbacks: No material caveats or missing details identified; the evidence is comprehensive and directly addresses all signals.
criteria.rationale: No material caveats or missing details identified; the evidence is comprehensive and directly addresses all signals.

evidence.text:
This proposal was developed in collaboration with SMA parents, carers and patients of Great Ormond
Street Hospital (GOSH) and charity partners. Medical colleagues from across the United Kingdom who
are linked to SMA were also consulted. Project proposal/relevance, study design and concerns were
shared.
A patient and communities’ group of 6 parents, 3 patients and 2 charity partners, aged 16-42 from
diverse socio-economic and ethnic backgrounds, were consulted on the plain English summary and
joined planning discussions. The Child and Young Person Group at GOSH contributed to the original
planning phase and plain English summary. The medical community stressed the important link between
the airway and lung volumes, and monitoring of patient tolerance to new tests. They highlighted this
work as visionary and a way to anticipate impact of the new phenotypes of SMA
The families confirmed importance of the research, highlighting it would help children stay out of
hospital, and their feedback has informed proposal development and design. Families reported it ‘gives
us a door to open up and see what is happening’ and ‘we are grateful you are looking into respiratory
care in SMA’.
Some par

ents raised concerns, and we revised/added activities to accommodate their feedback:
Transnasal fibreoptic laryngoscope (TFL): Concerns were regarding appearance, feel and what happens
if the child dislikes it. A child friendly video/cartoon explaining the procedure was suggested to help with
understanding. This has since been created. Patients requested to see the new equipment and all
equipment detailed in the study will be accessible to all patients.
Fatigue: The community requested testing for the TFL be completed in the morning, followed by the
opto-electronic plethysmography in the afternoon. We have ensured families will be able to take regular
breaks. The data collection period was made longer to allow for more spacing of patients to enable more
time per test.
Study Feedback: Regular feedback on the study was requested, ensuring it was appropriate and all-
inclusive. Coffee mornings/online meetings on study progression are planned.
Managing the Research
A communities’ group will be created, made up of 3 young adults living with SMA, 4 parents of children
with SMA and 3 representatives from charity partners. Recruitment will be through email
correspondence and social media,

criteria.name: Diversity and Inclusivity of the Research Team
criteria.definition: Extent to which the research team reflects diversity in terms of skills, experience, and demographic representation.

signals.signal_text: Explicit mentions of team composition across gender, ethnicity, age, or other relevant characteristics
Statements about inclusion in hiring or team-building
Evidence of equitable task allocation and collaboration

criteria.pros: The PPI group is explicitly described as diverse, including 6 parents, 3 patients, and 2 charity partners aged 16-42 from diverse socio-economic and ethnic backgrounds. This directly supports the representation of diversity within the PPI team.
criteria.drawbacks: While the PPI group is diverse, there is limited explicit evidence regarding the diversity of the core research team (supervisors/staff) beyond the PPI contributors. Statements about inclusion in hiring or equitable task allocation within the research team itself are not explicitly detailed.
criteria.rationale: While the PPI group is diverse, there is limited explicit evidence regarding the diversity of the core research team (supervisors/staff) beyond the PPI contributors. Statements about inclusion in hiring or equitable task allocation within the research team itself are not explicitly detailed.

criteria.name: Commitment to Maximising Public and Patient Benefit
criteria.definition: Clarity and strength of the plan to ensure research outcomes meaningfully benefit patients, carers, and the public.

signals.signal_text: Mentions of dissemination, knowledge translation, or implementation plans
Clear links between study objectives and anticipated benefits
Emphasis on actionable outcomes or improvements in health/care

criteria.pros: Dissemination plans are highly detailed and actionable, including podcasts, meetings with parent support groups, and charity networks. There are clear links between study objectives (optimising MI-E) and anticipated benefits (reduced hospitalisations, improved quality of life). The steering group is tasked with assisting in writing final reports and facilitating publication to ensure findings reach all audiences.
criteria.drawbacks: No material caveats identified; the plan is strong and well-evidenced.
criteria.rationale: No material caveats identified; the plan is strong and well-evidenced.

evidence.text:
Knowledge mobilisation, dissemination, and impact
Improved secretion management will reduce hospitalisations improving health and wellbeing. The study
provides insight into airway dynamics and will translate into other paediatric conditions. Results will be
shared via podcasts and meetings with parent support groups/charities, and presentations at
meetings/conferences, throughout the UK and internationally, including submission to appropriate peer
reviewed journals.
Research Inclusion
The study focuses on paediatric patients with SMA, which as a rare disease serves an under-
represented population from all cultural backgrounds. It is supported by community steering groups.
Adjustments have been made to ensure patients with behavioural and communicative concerns are able
to participate. Patient information sheets will be available to all and adapted for different communication
styles. An animated video has been created to aid inclusion. Characteristics related to inclusion of
participants will be reported. Ongoing reviews will ensure equality, diversity and inclusion are
considered.

ces in breathing patterns. This will highlight
any changes in the way children with SMA breath, having potential benefit in clearing more mucus out
from the lungs.
Part 3. Understand child's', parents' and carers' views, tolerance, and acceptance of the new
assessment techniques.
During the tests, children will be asked to rate the new tests in terms of acceptance, comfort, and
tolerance on a visual scale.
Sharing results
We will include children and their parents during the study. They will guide us in the best ways to share
information about what we find. We will also share what we find at meetings and conferences (In the
United Kingdom and internationally). We will write about what we learnt and share this through medical
and physiotherapy journals.

evidence.text:
with a follow up meeting to discuss role. Face to face and online
meetings were requested. There will be yearly face to face meetings and online groups 5-6 monthly.
Discussions via emails/telephone calls will occur at key timepoints.
Parents/Patients/Charity Partners will be offered to:
• Participate in training for involvement in advisory groups, provided by Imperial College
• Guide the project throughout the research process
• Explore ethical issues
• Assist with plain English summaries/updates
• Provide patient representation, parent perspective and SMA network links.
International and national multi-disciplinary professionals will form a steering group, focusing on ethical
issues/study design/time management/concerns arising throughout the project.
Dissemination
Steering groups will assist with writing final reports/summaries of results and will be acknowledge for
their involvement. Their role in facilitating publication of findings on charity/SMA specific websites and
dissemination through their informal networks will be invaluable to ensuring dissemination to all
audiences.

criteria.name: Representation of Seldom Heard or Underserved Groups
criteria.definition: Degree to which participants and PPI contributors represent groups historically underrepresented in research, including those facing health inequalities.

signals.signal_text: Mention of specific demographic groups (age, race, ethnicity, disability, gender, sexual orientation, religion/belief, location, socioeconomic status)
Evidence of targeted recruitment strategies
Discussion of barriers to participation and mitigation strategies

criteria.pros: The application explicitly focuses on Spinal Muscular Atrophy (SMA), a rare disease affecting an under-represented population. The PPI group includes participants from diverse socio-economic and ethnic backgrounds. Adjustments for behavioural and communicative concerns are noted to ensure participation.
criteria.drawbacks: While barriers like fatigue and communication are addressed, a more detailed discussion of specific recruitment strategies to overcome barriers for other underserved subgroups (e.g., specific geographic or socioeconomic barriers beyond the PPI group composition) could be more explicit.
criteria.rationale: While barriers like fatigue and communication are addressed, a more detailed discussion of specific recruitment strategies to overcome barriers for other underserved subgroups (e.g., specific geographic or socioeconomic barriers beyond the PPI group composition) could be more explicit.

criteria.name: Inclusivity Across the Research Lifecycle
criteria.definition: Demonstration that inclusion is considered at all stages of the project: planning, recruitment, conduct, analysis, and dissemination.

signals.signal_text: References to inclusion in study design, participant selection, and analysis
Consideration of accessibility and equity in all project stages
Documented plans for ongoing evaluation and adaptation

criteria.pros: Inclusion is demonstrated across the entire lifecycle: planning (co-design of PIS and methods), recruitment (adaptations for communication styles, animated videos), conduct (scheduling for fatigue, breaks), and dissemination (steering group involvement in reporting). Ongoing reviews for equality and diversity are planned.
criteria.drawbacks: No material caveats identified; the evidence covers all stages of the research lifecycle comprehensively.
criteria.rationale: No material caveats identified; the evidence covers all stages of the research lifecycle comprehensively.

Use of bold or emphasis for key terms
Structured hierarchy of sections (logical nesting of headings)
Patterns detected via text parsing for formatting markers

sub_criteria.pros: The application form analysis confirms the presence of 6 distinct sections with clear labels (Scientific Abstract, Plain English Summary, etc.) and 18 list markers, indicating strong use of headings and subheadings. The parser detected 21 emphasis markers and 1 numbered heading-like line, supporting structured hierarchy. The section order follows a logical progression from context to budget.
sub_criteria.drawbacks: The analysis notes a parser limitation where bold/emphasis markers may be lost during text extraction; while 21 markers were extracted, the reliability of emphasis usage (af.1.b) is slightly reduced by this technical caveat. The single numbered heading-like line suggests limited use of numbered sectioning compared to bullet points.
sub_criteria.rationale: The analysis notes a parser limitation where bold/emphasis markers may be lost during text extraction; while 21 markers were extracted, the reliability of emphasis usage (af.1.b) is slightly reduced by this technical caveat. The single numbered heading-like line suggests limited use of numbered sectioning compared to bullet points.
